Side effects?Yes: text_/variables really growAlmost none: only records structure
ProductFinal text + named results s["name"]Program structure + static prefix (cacheable/reusable)
When usedActually running a programPrecompute shared prefix, compile/optimize

The state object s: where the interpreter stores results

Either way, everything revolves around the state object s (ProgramState). In interpret mode, StreamExecutor is the "ledger" behind s: it accumulates the full text text_ (this is precisely the "prefix" for later requests), records role messages into messages_, and stores each gen/select result by name into variables. So when you write s["reason"] in Python, you're reading variables["reason"]. The diagram below draws "what gets added to the state when one gen completes":

What ProgramState holds: each step the interpreter runs drops things into s's three baskets — text, messages, named variables
s += sgl.user("Q…")append to text_→messages_ logs a user turn
gen("reason")backend generates text→append to text_+variables["reason"]
read s["reason"]= variables["reason"]→used in plain Python

A peek at the source: StreamExecutor

The heart of the interpreter is StreamExecutor. In a background thread it holds the backend, the variables table, the full text text_, and more; submit drops an expression into the queue, the worker pops it and _executes, and _execute_gen is where the model is actually called and the result is written back into text_ and variables. Here is its real skeleton:

python/sglang/lang/interpreter.py ::StreamExecutorinterpreter: execute per-expression, write gen results back to state
class StreamExecutor:
    """A stream executor that executes SGL expressions in a background thread."""

    def __init__(self, backend, arguments, ...):
        self.backend = backend          # the chosen backend (Lesson 12)
        self.variables = {}             # named results: s["name"] reads from here
        self.text_ = ""             # the ever-growing full text = the prefix
        self.messages_ = []             # role messages in OpenAI format
        # …a background worker thread consumes expressions from the queue…

    def submit(self, expr):             # drop a gen/select node into the queue
        self._init_var_event(expr)
        self.queue.put(expr)

    def _execute_gen(self, expr):       # where the model is actually called
        comp, meta_info = self.backend.generate(self, ...)
        self.text_ += comp              # append the generated result to the text
        self.variables[expr.name] = comp  # store into a named variable for s["name"]
python/sglang/lang/interpreter.py ::StreamExecutorexecutes a DSL program node-by-node against a backend; supports fork parallelism
class StreamExecutor:
    # executes a DSL program against a backend, one IR node at a time
    def __init__(self, backend, ...):
        self.backend = backend
        self.variables = {}        # name -> generated value
    def submit(self, expr):
        ...   # enqueue an IR node (gen / select / fill ...) to execute
    def fork(self, size=1):
        ...   # split into parallel branches that share the current prefix
    def _execute_gen(self, expr):
        ...   # call the backend to actually generate the tokens

Concretely: fork(3) splits the current point into 3 parallel branches at once; they share the same parent prefix and each call the backend independently. And the variables{} table holds every gen result — your s["reason"] reads exactly variables["reason"].

Read this and you grasp the interpreter's soul: it is a loop that "consumes expressions one by one and keeps accumulating results into state." Fixed text is appended; gen/select hits the backend and writes back into text_ and variables — that's how state grows step by step. The tracer walks the same tree but swaps "hit the backend" for a symbolic placeholder, so it can see the structure and extract the prefix without spending a single FLOP. Carry "interpret = execute and accumulate state; trace = symbolically read the shape and extract the prefix" forward: Lesson 11 shows how the interpreter runs branches fork/join concurrently when there's no data dependency and lets them share the parent prefix's KV (Lesson 7); Lesson 12 unveils where gen actually lands — the backend interface and OpenAI compatibility.

📌 Key points
  • One tree, two ways to run: the same @sgl.function tree can be interpreted (executed) or traced (analyzed).
  • Interpret = StreamExecutor: a background thread _executes per expression; fixed text is appended to text_, gen/select calls the backend (Lesson 12), results stream onto text_ and into variables[name]; between two gens is plain Python.
  • Trace = trace_program: a symbolic walk with a dummy backend, never calling the model, discovering structure and extracting the static prefix (the constant leading run, stopping at the first gen), for the runtime to precompute/cache/share.
  • Why two modes: interpret answers "what to produce now"; trace answers "what shape is this program" — extracting the shared prefix lets the runtime compute it once and reuse the KV (Lesson 7, RadixAttention) and enables compilation/optimization.
  • What ProgramState s holds: text_ (full text/prefix), messages_ (role messages), variables (named results, read via s["name"]).
  • Where it fits: DSL primitives (Lesson 9) → interpret/trace (this lesson) → fork/join concurrency (Lesson 11) → backend landing (Lesson 12).

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. In SGLang, what is the most essential difference between interpret and trace?
  1. Interpret really drives the backend step by step via StreamExecutor, generates tokens, and mutates state; trace walks symbolically with a dummy backend, never calls the model, and only discovers structure and extracts the static prefix
  2. Both call the model; trace is merely faster
  3. Interpret is for training, trace is for evaluation
  4. Interpret only runs on GPU, trace only runs on CPU
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. Interpret is the normal execution path: StreamExecutor _executes per expression, gen/select really hits the backend, appends to text_ and stores into variables, growing state step by step. Trace is the compile/analysis path: trace_program walks symbolically with a dummy backend, never touching the model, only discovering structure and extracting the constant leading static prefix. The others misstate the mechanism.
2. Why does trace walk the program 'without calling the model'? What is its most valuable product?
  1. To see the program's structure without spending compute, and to extract the 'static prefix' (the constant leading prompt up to the first place that depends on a model output), so the runtime can compute it once and reuse its KV
  2. To compress model weights and save HBM
  3. To pre-cache every gen's answer to disk
  4. To lower sampling temperature for more deterministic output
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. Trace aims to 'understand the shape first': a symbolic walk with a dummy backend that stops at any node depending on a model result, so it discovers structure and extracts the constant leading static prefix at zero compute. The runtime then computes attention for that shared prefix once and reuses its KV (Lesson 7, RadixAttention), and can compile/optimize from it. It has nothing to do with compressing weights, caching answers, or temperature.
3. In interpret mode, what does the state object s (ProgramState) actually hold, and what does s["name"] read?
  1. It stores pointers to the model weights
  2. It only stores a boolean flag for whether the program finished
  3. It accumulates the full text text_ (the prefix), role messages messages_, and named results stored in variables; s["name"] reads variables["name"] — the output of some gen/select
  4. It encodes everything into a single integer token id
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. StreamExecutor is the 'ledger' behind s: text_ is the ever-growing full text (also the prefix for later requests), messages_ logs role messages, and variables stores each gen/select result by name. So s["reason"] in Python reads variables["reason"]. The others don't match the source fields.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • In your own words, describe what StreamExecutor does — from being called to producing the final text — when you invoke an @sgl.function with two gens. Point out how each gen hits the backend, how results are written back into text_ and variables, and where s["name"] reads from.
  • Suppose your service has ten thousand requests all starting with the same long system prompt + few-shot template. Argue why running a 'trace' once to extract the static prefix helps the runtime's throughput. How does it connect to Lesson 7's RadixAttention prefix reuse? Where does tracing stop, and why?

fork/join & prefix sharing

Last lesson (Lesson 10) showed how the interpreter runs a tree of intent step by step. But what really sets SGLang apart is that a program can fork multiple parallel branches off the current state — that is s.fork(n). The one line to remember today: the front-end's fork and the runtime's RadixAttention (Lesson 7) are two halves of one idea. fork lets you declare "branch here" in the program; RadixAttention computes that branch's shared prefix once in the engine and reuses it for every branch. One states the structure, the other cashes it in as saved compute. Grasp this correspondence and you finally understand the weight of the word Structured in SGLang's name.

🔌 Analogy
fork/join is like a choose-your-own-adventure book: every reader first reads the same opening chapters (the shared prefix); at a fork in the road the book says "to save the princess turn to page 50, to slay the dragon turn to page 80" — so one story branches into several different endings. The key: the shared opening is printed only once, never re-copied for each branch; each reader only reads their own short second half. Finally you lay the endings side by side and pick the best — that is join (gathering each branch's result back). fork = branch at the shared opening, join = aggregate the separately-obtained answers.
🌍 The big picture
In one line: s.fork(n) clones n parallel continuation branches from the current state, each inheriting the same prompt prefix built so far, then each generates independently (e.g. n different samples, or n different sub-questions); join / gather then collects each branch's newly-captured variables back into the parent state for you to compare, vote, or aggregate. Its power is not convenience but saved compute: since the n branches share an identical prefix, RadixAttention computes that prefix's KV once and shares it across all branches (Lesson 7). So forking 8 branches off a long prefix costs roughly "1 prefix + 8 very short suffixes," not "8 full prompts." fork is therefore not just a convenience — it is what "prefix sharing" looks like in the programming model.

What fork does: N branches grown from one state

Think of state s as an ever-growing dialogue record (Lessons 9, 10). At some moment you have built up a long prompt into s — system setup, few-shot examples, the user question. Calling s.fork(n) now clones n identical copies at the current tail of that record, each carrying the entire prefix so far. The n branches then go their separate ways: branch 0 may sample at one temperature, branch 1 at another; or you append a different sub-question to each. They are independent and do not interfere. Once all branches finish, you call join (default gather_variable mode), and the framework copies each branch's newly-written named variables back into the parent state s — e.g. a variable in the parent becomes a list holding all n branches' answers, ready to compare, vote, or pick the best.

1

Build the shared prefix

First assemble system + few-shot + question into s — this segment is shared by all branches and is the root of fork's savings.

2

s.fork(n): branch in place

Clone n parallel sub-states from the current state, each inheriting the same prefix; returns a ProgramStateGroup.

3

Each branch generates independently

Each branch forks[i] += gen("answer") writes its own; branches with no data dependency can run concurrently (interpreter, Lesson 10).

4

join: gather answers back

forks.join() aggregates each branch's new variables back into s, so s["answer"] becomes a list of n results to compare / vote / aggregate.

Fig 1 · fork shares the prefix — one shared prefix (system + few-shot + question) is split in place by s.fork(3) into 3 branches: the prefix KV is computed once, shared by all 3 branches, and each branch computes only its own short suffix.

Concretely: a prefix of about 2000 tokens (system + few-shot + question), then s.fork(3) and each branch writes only ~100 tokens. Running it serially 3 times costs 3×2000 + 3×100 ≈ 6300 tokens; with fork it costs only 2000 + 3×100 ≈ 2300 — saving roughly 4000 tokens of duplicated prefix compute.

The core: fork and RadixAttention are two halves of one idea

This is the part to highlight hardest. Lesson 7 explained: RadixAttention organizes all requests' prefixes into a radix tree, where a common prefix stores one copy of KV shared by many requests. What fork does is exactly deliberately create a "one common prefix + many different suffixes" shape in the program. In other words: fork is the "declaration side," RadixAttention is the "redemption side." Writing s.fork(8) in the DSL tells the runtime plainly: "these 8 branches have an identical opening." The runtime takes that signal and computes the shared prefix's attention only once; the 8 branches share one prefix KV, each adding compute only for its own short suffix. Without fork's declaration, RadixAttention could still discover after the fact that prefixes match; with fork, the sharing is structurally explicit, zero-guess.

Shared prefix computed once, each branch adds a short suffix: highlighted cells are the same prefix KV shared by all forked branches (Lesson 7 RadixAttention reuse); white cells are what each branch computes anew
Branch 0systemfew-shotquestion→answer A
Branch 1systemfew-shotquestion→answer B
Branch 2systemfew-shotquestion→answer C
reusethe three highlighted runs = one prefix KV stored once in the radix tree, shared by all three branches
Fig 2 · join merges branch results — the 3 branches each produce a candidate answer; join (gather_variable) collects them back into the parent state s, so s["answer"] becomes the list [A, B, C] to compare / vote / pick the best.

Serial loop vs fork: where the cost differs

For "best-of-n," the naïve way is a for loop running n times: each iteration resends the full prompt. The problem: those n requests share an identical long prefix, yet it gets recomputed n times end to end — burning (n−1) prefixes of compute, and the iterations run serially, blind to one another. With s.fork(n), you declare the "shared prefix + n suffixes" structure once, the runtime computes one prefix plus n short suffixes, and independent branches can run concurrently. The longer the prefix and the larger n, the more you save.

Serial for loop: N × full prompt

Run n gens in a loop, each recomputing the whole long prefix. Cost ≈ n × (prefix + suffix), prefix recomputed n times; iterations serial, runtime can't tell their openings match.

s.fork(n): 1 × prefix + N × suffix

Declare the fork once; the runtime computes the shared prefix KV only once (Lesson 7), n branches each add only a short suffix. Cost ≈ prefix + n × suffix; independent branches also run concurrently (Lesson 10).

Common uses: what fork is good for

Whenever a task can be phrased as "share an opening, then do several things separately," fork applies. The four patterns below are the most common — all share the trait of branching off the same parent prefix, so all automatically enjoy prefix reuse.

PatternHow it forksWhat join does next
best-of-n samplingfork n branches off one prompt, each generating n candidates with different randomnessgather n answers, score / vote for the best
branch-and-evaluategenerate several distinct options, one branch per optionevaluate each option independently, then aggregate to compare
map over a listopen one branch per element, sharing the same instruction prefixcollect each element's result into a list
tree-of-thoughtat each thinking node fork several reasoning paths, forking again layer by layerexplore / prune down the tree, recover the best path

One more word on concurrency: when forked branches have no data dependency, the interpreter (Lesson 10) can advance them concurrently rather than waiting for one to finish before the next. So fork brings two benefits at once: compute-wise, RadixAttention saves the duplicated prefix; time-wise, concurrency overlaps the branches. Now the real fork in the source.

python/sglang/lang/interpreter.py ::ProgramState.forkfork: branch N sub-states off the current state
def fork(
    self,
    size: int = 1,
    position_ids_offset: Optional[List[int]] = None,
):
    # Have the executor clone `size` branches from the current state — they share the built prefix
    stream_executors = self.stream_executor.fork(size, position_ids_offset)
    states = [ProgramState(x) for x in stream_executors]
    # Pack into a group; later group.join() copies each branch's variables back to the parent
    state_group = ProgramStateGroup(states, self)
    return state_group

A few lines, but they tie the two halves together: stream_executor.fork(size, …) does the real "clone size branches from the current prefix" at the runtime layer — exactly the moment RadixAttention's prefix tree grows size children off a shared parent node; the returned ProgramStateGroup gives you join, which in gather_variable mode copies each branch's new variables back into the parent state. With one s.fork(n), front-end declaration and runtime reuse become one and the same. Lesson 12 next covers the backend interface, landing this fork/join onto concrete engines — completing Part 3's chain of "language → interpret → fork → land."

python/sglang/lang/interpreter.py ::ProgramStatethe handle s a DSL program receives: fork, join, read generated variables
class ProgramState:
    # the handle a DSL program function receives (the `s` in def prog(s, ...))
    def __init__(self, stream_executor):
        self.stream_executor = stream_executor
    def fork(self, size=1):
        ...   # branch into `size` parallel states that share the current prefix
    def text(self):
        ...   # the accumulated text so far
    def __getitem__(self, name):
        ...   # read a generated variable, e.g. s["answer"]

Putting the pieces together gives the most common usage: forks = s.fork(3) yields 3 branches sharing the prefix, for f in forks: f += gen("answer") lets each write its own, then forks.join() gathers them back — and s["answer"] (going through the __getitem__ above) becomes a candidate list like ["answer A", "answer B", "answer C"].

📌 Key points
  • What fork does: s.fork(n) branches n parallel continuations off the current state, each inheriting the same already-built prefix, then generating independently; join (gather) copies each branch's new named variables back into the parent for compare / vote / aggregate.
  • The core correspondence: fork is the "declaration side," RadixAttention (Lesson 7) the "redemption side" — two halves of one idea. fork states "these branches share an opening"; the runtime computes the shared prefix's KV once and reuses it for every branch.
  • Cost: forking 8 branches off a long prefix ≈ 1 prefix + 8 short suffixes, not 8 full prompts; a serial for loop recomputes the prefix n times. The longer the prefix and larger n, the more you save.
  • Common uses: best-of-n sampling, branch-and-evaluate, map over a list, tree-of-thought — all share "share an opening, then do things separately."
  • Concurrency: branches with no data dependency can be advanced concurrently by the interpreter (Lesson 10). So fork saves compute (prefix reuse) and time (concurrency) at once. Source: interpreter.py ::ProgramState.fork; Lesson 12 lands it on concrete backends.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. When you call s.fork(n) on a state, what exactly do the n branches share?
  1. They share one random seed, so they would necessarily produce identical text
  2. They all inherit the same prompt prefix built up to the fork point (system + few-shot + question, etc.), then generate independently afterward
  3. They share a dedicated GPU that no other request may use
  4. They share nothing; fork is equivalent to firing n brand-new requests from scratch
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. fork clones n sub-states from the current state, each carrying the same prefix built so far, after which they generate independently. Precisely because the prefix is identical, RadixAttention (Lesson 7) computes it once and reuses it across branches. Branches with different randomness yield different results (not necessarily identical); no dedicated GPU is involved; and it is not from scratch.
2. This lesson repeatedly stresses that 'fork and RadixAttention are two halves of one idea.' What does that most precisely mean?
  1. fork is the 'declaration side' — it states in the program that 'these branches share an opening'; RadixAttention is the 'redemption side' — it computes that shared prefix's KV once and reuses it across branches. The front-end declares structure, the runtime cashes it in as saved compute
  2. fork only affects front-end readability and has no effect on runtime compute
  3. fork turns RadixAttention off and switches to disk caching
  4. They are mutually exclusive optimizations; enabling fork disables prefix reuse
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. fork explicitly states in the DSL that 'these n branches share one prefix,' telling the runtime the structure with zero guessing; RadixAttention takes that signal and computes the shared prefix's attention once, with all branches sharing one KV. One states the structure, the other cashes it in. They cooperate rather than exclude, and neither disables the other nor degrades to disk caching.
3. Forking 8 branches off a long shared prefix, versus a for loop running 8 full prompts serially — roughly how does the cost differ?
  1. The serial loop is cheaper because it never runs concurrently
  2. The costs are identical; fork is merely shorter to write
  3. fork is more expensive because it must store 8 copies of the prefix KV
  4. fork ≈ '1 prefix + 8 short suffixes' (prefix computed once); the serial loop ≈ '8 × full prompt' (prefix recomputed 8 times). The longer the prefix, the more fork saves, and independent branches can also run concurrently
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. In the serial loop, the same long prefix opens all 8 requests yet is recomputed 8 times, wasting 7 prefixes of compute; fork declares 'shared prefix + 8 suffixes' once, so the runtime computes one prefix KV shared by all 8 branches, each adding only a short suffix, with concurrency when independent. fork does not store 8 prefixes (it stores exactly one), and the costs are far from identical.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • Write best-of-n pseudocode in SGLang: build a longish system prompt + question into s, then forks = s.fork(8) so 8 branches each do gen("answer"), finally forks.join() to gather 8 answers. Explain which segment the 8 branches share, which segment each computes independently, and what the runtime (RadixAttention, Lesson 7) thereby computes only once.
  • Someone claims 'fork is just convenient syntactic sugar; drop it and a for loop does the same.' Rebut this using the 'fork is the declaration side, RadixAttention the redemption side' relationship: why does explicitly declaring the branch structure benefit both prefix reuse and concurrency at the runtime? And what role does join play in all this?

Backends & OpenAI compat

From Lesson 9 through Lesson 11 we have been writing programs: describing intent in the DSL (Lesson 9), letting the interpreter run the tree of intent (Lesson 10), forking parallel branches with fork/join (Lesson 11). But one question has been quietly dodged all along — who actually executes all these gen, select, fork calls? The answer is today's star: the Backend. The heart of SGLang's design is that the same front-end program runs unchanged on different backends — the local SGLang runtime, OpenAI, Anthropic, VertexAI… and the program itself has no idea which backend it runs on. This lesson closes Part 3 (the front-end) and, through the RuntimeEndpoint local-runtime backend, snaps seamlessly onto the runtime world of Part 4+: your fork/gen become HTTP calls to the scheduler we study next.

🔌 Analogy
A backend interface is like a universal remote: one set of buttons (power, channel, volume) drives any TV — the living-room one, the bedroom one, a friend's. When you press "channel," you needn't care what brand sits in front of you. But there is a key difference: only your own TV (the local runtime) exposes the advanced features — picture enhancement, recording, scheduling (i.e. prefix cache, constrained decoding, branch sharing); a generic stranger's TV (a hosted API) only knows the basic buttons. "One button set drives all TVs" maps to the BaseBackend abstract interface; "only your own TV has the advanced keys" maps to: only the local runtime gives you RadixAttention and constrained decoding, while hosted models are black boxes — whether they cache, whether they strictly constrain, is not up to you. Remember this remote and you remember the whole lesson: one interface, layered capabilities.
🌍 The big picture
In one line: BaseBackend is an abstract interface that prescribes what a backend must be able to do — generate, select (pick among candidates), streaming, role handling, and so on. Your DSL program calls only these abstract methods and never touches a concrete engine directly. So switching backends is one line, set_default_backend(...): pass RuntimeEndpoint("http://localhost:30000") to run on the local SGLang runtime (the /generate HTTP server we build in Lessons 13–17), pass OpenAI(...) and the same program targets OpenAI's hosted models instead. But there is no free lunch: the local runtime gives you RadixAttention prefix caching (Lesson 7), constrained decoding, fast batching; a hosted backend buys you portability at the cost of losing prefix-cache and fork-sharing benefits — those models are black boxes, caching is the provider's call, and constrained decoding is limited.
Fig 1 · One program, swappable backend — the middle DSL program is written once: point set_default_backend at the local RuntimeEndpoint on the left to run on your own server, or at remote OpenAI/Anthropic on the right to target a hosted model — the program itself does not change a single character.

1. BaseBackend: the seam that decouples program from engine

Recall Lesson 10: the interpreter (StreamExecutor) executes the expressions of the intent tree one by one — fixed text is appended, and a gen / select issues a call to the backend. That "backend" is any class inheriting from BaseBackend. BaseBackend abstracts "an engine that can execute an SGLang program" into a set of methods: how to generate tokens, how to select among candidates, how to stream, how to handle system/user/assistant roles. The program always faces this interface, never a specific engine. This is the classic "program to an interface": the upper layer depends on a contract, not on the lower layer's implementation. So the same intent tree can run on local GPUs today and a cloud API tomorrow, with not one character changed.

Program@sgl.function intent tree
Your DSL gen/select/fork (Lessons 9–11). It calls only abstract methods and has no idea which backend it runs on.
InterfaceBaseBackend (abstract)
Prescribes what a backend must do: generate / select, streaming, role handling. This seam decouples program from engine.
LocalRuntimeEndpoint (preferred)
Targets the local /generate HTTP server (Lessons 13–17). Exclusively gives RadixAttention prefix cache, constrained decoding, fast batching.
HostedOpenAI / Anthropic / VertexAI …
Points the same program at hosted models: portable, but the model is a black box, losing prefix-cache and fork-sharing benefits.
Fig 2 · The backend interface — BaseBackend defines methods like generate/select; the DSL interpreter calls only this interface, while RuntimeEndpoint (local HTTP) and OpenAI (remote API) plug in as two implementations, each replaceable by the other.

2. RuntimeEndpoint: the seam where the front-end lands on the runtime

Among all backends, RuntimeEndpoint matters most — it is the seam where Part 3 (front-end) snaps precisely onto Part 4+ (runtime). It points at a locally launched SGLang server (default http://localhost:30000) and translates every gen / fork in the program into a POST /generate HTTP request, sent to the server we dissect layer by layer from Lesson 13 onward — the TokenizerManager at the door (Lesson 14), the Scheduler as the brain (Lessons 18–23), the ModelRunner doing the forward (Lesson 24). Precisely because the request lands on our own runtime, the RadixAttention prefix cache (Lesson 7), fork's branch sharing (Lesson 11), and constrained decoding actually take effect: the 8 branches you fork share one prefix and the runtime computes it once — something only the RuntimeEndpoint backend can deliver. One line, set_default_backend(RuntimeEndpoint("http://localhost:30000")), welds the front-end's full expressiveness onto the very engine we are about to open up.

Program
s += gen("answer")
→
RuntimeEndpoint
local-runtime backend
→
POST /generate
HTTP request · Lessons 13–17
→
Scheduler
schedule + RadixAttention (Lesson 7)
python/sglang/lang/backend/runtime_endpoint.py ::RuntimeEndpointa backend talking to a local SGLang server over HTTP: /generate, /select
class RuntimeEndpoint(BaseBackend):
    # a backend that talks to a running SGLang server over HTTP
    def __init__(self, base_url, ...):
        self.base_url = base_url
    def generate(self, s, sampling_params):
        ...   # POST base_url + "/generate"
    def select(self, s, choices, ...):
        ...   # score the choices and pick the best one

In code it is just one line — switching backends changes only this: set_default_backend(RuntimeEndpoint("http://localhost:30000")) runs the same program on the local runtime; change it to set_default_backend(OpenAI("gpt-4")) and it targets a hosted model instead, with the rest of the program untouched.

3. Local runtime vs hosted API: what are you trading?

Switching backends is not free. RuntimeEndpoint and OpenAI look identical in the program, but the capabilities you get differ vastly. The crux: the local runtime is a white box you can see through and control, so the prefix cache (Lesson 7), fork sharing (Lesson 11), and strict constrained decoding (guaranteeing valid JSON / regex output) are all available; while a hosted model is a black box — the provider may cache internally, but you cannot declare or rely on it, and constrained decoding usually degrades to "begging for it in the prompt." Here is the trade laid out.

Local runtime (RuntimeEndpoint)

White box, controllable: full RadixAttention prefix cache (Lesson 7), fork branch sharing (Lesson 11), strict constrained decoding, fast batching. The cost: you must deploy and manage GPUs yourself (Lessons 13–17 teach you). For performance and control, use this.

Hosted API (OpenAI / Anthropic …)

Black box, portable: one line switches to a top closed model, no GPUs needed. The cost: you lose the deterministic prefix-cache and fork-sharing benefits, constrained decoding is limited, and you pay per token. For convenience and portability, use this.

4. Don't mix the two directions: who calls whose OpenAI API

"OpenAI-compatible" is the most confusing phrase because it involves two opposite directions — keep them straight:

DirectionWho is client / who is serverMeaning
SGLang program → OpenAI backendyour program is the client, OpenAI is the serverUse the OpenAI(...) backend so your SGLang program calls OpenAI's hosted models
OpenAI client → SGLang serversomeone's program is the client, your SGLang is the serverThe SGLang server is itself OpenAI-API-compatible; any OpenAI client can point straight at it

In other words: SGLang can be a client calling OpenAI (direction one), and a server called by OpenAI clients (direction two). The second direction is especially handy — the SGLang server you stand up in Lessons 13–17 exposes a standard OpenAI interface, so any existing OpenAI SDK, LangChain, or third-party tool plugs into your private deployment by just changing base_url, without rewriting a single line of client code for SGLang. One "compatibility," two directions: a bridge both to consume others' models and to plug your own service into the whole ecosystem.

python/sglang/lang/backend/runtime_endpoint.py ::RuntimeEndpointlocal-runtime backend: every generate POSTs /generate
class RuntimeEndpoint(BaseBackend):
    def __init__(self, base_url, api_key=None, ...):
        super().__init__()
        self.base_url = base_url
        # Startup handshake: ask the local runtime which model is loaded
        res = http_request(self.base_url + "/get_model_info", ...)
        self.model_info = res.json()

    def cache_prefix(self, prefix_str):
        # Send the shared prefix with max_new_tokens=0 to pre-warm RadixAttention (Lesson 7)
        http_request(self.base_url + "/generate",
            json={"text": prefix_str, "sampling_params": {"max_new_tokens": 0}})

    def generate(self, s, sampling_params):
        # A gen in the program becomes one POST /generate HTTP call here
        data = {"text": s.text_, "sampling_params": {...}}
        res = http_request(self.base_url + "/generate", json=data)
        return res.json()["text"], res.json()["meta_info"]  # completion text + meta

These few lines are the door from front-end to runtime: the constructor handshakes via /get_model_info to confirm the model; cache_prefix uses max_new_tokens=0 to pre-warm the shared prefix into the cache, the very mechanism behind fork's compute savings (Lesson 11); and generate faithfully translates each gen in the program into one POST /generate. Understand this and you understand the seam between Part 3 and Part 4 — from the next lesson on, we step into the server behind that /generate, watching TokenizerManager, Scheduler, and ModelRunner turn this one HTTP request into real tokens on the GPU.

📌 Key points
  • One interface: BaseBackend is the abstract layer prescribing the contract — generate/select, streaming, role handling. The DSL program (Lessons 9–11) calls only abstract methods and neither knows nor cares which backend runs it — switching is one line, set_default_backend(...).
  • Local runtime: RuntimeEndpoint("http://localhost:30000") translates gen/fork into POST /generate to the server built in Lessons 13–17. Only it gives you RadixAttention prefix cache (Lesson 7), fork branch sharing (Lesson 11), constrained decoding, and fast batching.
  • Hosted backends: OpenAI / Anthropic / VertexAI point the same program at closed models — portable, no self-hosted GPU, but you lose prefix-cache and fork-sharing (black-box model), constrained decoding is limited, and you pay per token.
  • Two directions, don't mix: ① SGLang program → OpenAI backend (your program calls OpenAI); ② OpenAI client → SGLang server (the SGLang server is itself OpenAI-compatible; any OpenAI client plugs into your private deployment by changing base_url).
  • Bridge forward: RuntimeEndpoint is the seam welding Part 3 (front-end) onto Part 4+ (runtime). Source in lang/backend/runtime_endpoint.py; from the next lesson we step into the server behind /generate.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. Run the same SGLang front-end program on two backends: RuntimeEndpoint (local runtime) and OpenAI (hosted). Which backend actually gives you RadixAttention prefix caching and fork branch sharing (Lessons 7, 11), and why?
  1. Both backends are identical; prefix caching has nothing to do with the backend
  2. The OpenAI backend, because closed models always cache better than local ones
  3. Neither; prefix caching can only be done manually in the prompt
  4. RuntimeEndpoint: it sends requests to your own local SGLang server (a white box), so the runtime sees the prefix structure and computes a shared prefix once; a hosted model is a black box where caching is the provider's call, which you can neither declare nor rely on
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. RadixAttention and fork sharing are runtime capabilities, effective only when the request actually lands on a local SGLang runtime you control (RuntimeEndpoint posting to /generate). A hosted API hides the model behind a black box, so you cannot declare the prefix structure or guarantee cache hits, losing that deterministic benefit. The two backends look identical in code but differ vastly in capability.
2. 'OpenAI-compatible' involves two opposite directions. Which option correctly distinguishes them?
  1. Direction ① 'SGLang program → OpenAI backend': your program is the client calling OpenAI's hosted models; direction ② 'OpenAI client → SGLang server': the SGLang server is itself OpenAI-compatible, so someone else's OpenAI client plugs into your private deployment by just changing base_url
  2. The two directions are the same thing — both mean SGLang calling OpenAI
  3. 'Compatible' refers only to identical data formats, independent of who calls whom
  4. Only OpenAI can be the server; SGLang is always the client
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. The directions are exactly opposite: in ① SGLang is the client and OpenAI the server (using the OpenAI backend to consume hosted models); in ② SGLang is the server and an OpenAI client is the client (the SGLang server exposes a standard OpenAI interface, so any OpenAI SDK/LangChain plugs in by changing base_url). One consumes others' models; the other plugs your own service into the ecosystem.
3. You decide to switch your production service from the local RuntimeEndpoint to the hosted OpenAI backend. Beyond a one-line set_default_backend, what do you mainly trade away?
  1. You sacrifice nothing; the hosted backend is strictly better on every dimension
  2. You gain portability and skip self-hosting GPUs, but sacrifice the deterministic prefix-cache and fork-sharing benefits and strict constrained decoding, and switch to per-token billing — because the hosted model is a black box whose internal optimizations you cannot control
  3. You sacrifice program readability and must rewrite the entire intent tree
  4. You only sacrifice startup speed; runtime capabilities are identical
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. The local runtime is a white box delivering full RadixAttention prefix caching (Lesson 7), fork sharing (Lesson 11), and strict constrained decoding; a hosted API is a black box where those deterministic benefits cannot be declared or relied upon, constrained decoding is limited, and you pay per token. The upside is portability and no GPU ops. The program itself is unchanged (the whole point of the BaseBackend abstraction), so what you trade is runtime capability and cost structure, not readability.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • In a sentence or two, explain to a colleague why switching backends for the same SGLang program takes only one line, set_default_backend. Point out the role of the BaseBackend abstract interface and why the program 'neither knows nor cares' which backend runs it.
  • RuntimeEndpoint is called 'the seam joining Part 3 (front-end) to Part 4+ (runtime).' Using the fact that it translates gen/fork into POST /generate, explain why only this backend can deliver RadixAttention prefix caching (Lesson 7) and fork branch sharing (Lesson 11), and which components from Lessons 13–17 will handle that HTTP request next.

Engine & HTTP server

Welcome to Part 4 — we finally step from the front-end DSL through the door of the runtime. This lesson answers the most basic question: through what entry point does your program feed requests into SGLang's engine? There are exactly two answers: the offline Engine (a pure in-process Python API) and the online HTTP server (a FastAPI app). Understanding these two doors — and that they share one runtime underneath — is the key to every later lesson.

🔌 Analogy
Picture a restaurant with two ways to order. One is the walk-in counter: you step to the window and call your order straight to the kitchen — no phone, no middleman. That's the offline Engine: an in-process call with the lowest overhead. The other is the phone-order hotline: you dial, give your address, and an operator relays the ticket to the kitchen. That's the online server: a layer of network and protocol, but anyone, any client, can order remotely. The point: there is only one kitchen. Counter or phone, the kitchen is identical — the server is just a "front desk that answers calls" bolted onto the same Engine.
🌍 Big picture
SGLang offers two paths to drive the runtime, both inheriting from the same abstract base EngineBase: the offline Engine is a plain Python object whose llm.generate(...) returns results directly — ideal for batch jobs, evaluation, and RL rollout (the trainer calls it in-process); the online server uses launch_server() to start a FastAPI/uvicorn app wrapping an Engine, exposing the native POST /generate plus OpenAI-compatible routes. The runtime underneath is identical: the server is just an HTTP wrapper around the Engine.
Fig 1 · Two entry points: Engine vs HTTP Server — on the left the offline Engine calls engine.generate() directly inside your Python process, no network; on the right the online server wraps the same Engine with FastAPI, exposing /generate and /v1/.... One core, two skins.

Offline Engine: a pure in-process Python API

The offline Engine is the barest entry: no HTTP, no port, no network. In your own Python script you import sglang as sgl, build a sgl.Engine(model_path=...), then call llm.generate(prompts, sampling_params) — results come back right away as Python dicts/lists. It all runs in one process (precisely: the main process also holds the TokenizerManager, with the scheduler and detokenizer as subprocesses — more on that soon). With no serialization, HTTP parsing, or network round-trip, the Engine is the lowest-latency, most embeddable way in.

Offline Engine

sgl.Engine(model_path=...) + .generate(...). Pure in-process call, no HTTP, no network round-trip. Best for batch inference, offline eval, RL rollout (the trainer calls it in-process, lowest overhead). Downside: usable only from Python in this process — no cross-machine/cross-language.

Online server

launch_server(server_args) starts a FastAPI app exposing an HTTP interface. Best for production serving, multi-client, language-agnostic access — any HTTP client connects. Cost: an extra network + serialization layer, plus ports, concurrency, and auth to manage.

Online server: wrap the Engine in HTTP

The online server starts with one command: python -m sglang.launch_server --model-path .... Internally it builds an Engine, then wraps it with FastAPI/uvicorn, exposing two kinds of routes: SGLang's native POST /generate, and a full set of OpenAI-compatible routes like /v1/chat/completions and /v1/completions (Lesson 15 covers the compat layer). So an incoming HTTP request is translated into a call on the inner Engine, and the result (optionally streamed) is written back. The table aligns the three entry points with "who uses each":

EntryShapeTypical user
sgl.Engine().generate()In-process Python call, no HTTPBatch scripts, eval, RL trainer (Lesson 51)
POST /generateSGLang's native HTTP interfaceFront-end RuntimeEndpoint, custom clients
/v1/chat/completionsOpenAI-compatible HTTP routeOpenAI SDK / LangChain ecosystem (Lesson 15)
python/sglang/srt/entrypoints/http_server.py ::generate_requestthe HTTP /generate route: hand to TokenizerManager, then stream or return once
@app.post("/generate")
async def generate_request(obj: GenerateReqInput, request: Request):
    # the HTTP entry: pass the parsed request to the TokenizerManager,
    # then stream results back (SSE) or return one JSON response
    ...

Two of the most common online calls. First, hit SGLang's native route directly: curl localhost:30000/generate -d '{"text": "The capital of France is", "sampling_params": {"max_new_tokens": 16}}'. Second, use the OpenAI-compatible route by POSTing to /v1/chat/completions (with messages and model in the body) — an off-the-shelf OpenAI SDK just points its base_url at your server to hit it (Lesson 15).

One runtime: the server is just a shell

This is the lesson's most important sentence: whichever door you enter, the runtime underneath is the same. After passing the FastAPI app, an HTTP request lands on an Engine; and inside that Engine are exactly the components we'll dissect lesson by lesson — TokenizerManager (Lesson 14, tokenizing and registering requests), Scheduler (Lesson 18, batching and scheduling), DetokenizerManager (detokenizing and streaming back). So everything from Lesson 14 onward is shared by both offline Engine and online server — you only learn it once.

HTTP request
/generate · /v1/chat/...
→
FastAPI app
shell from launch_server
→
Engine
shared runtime core
→
Three components
Tokenizer · Scheduler · Detokenizer
Fig 2 · Online request fan-out — many concurrent clients each send one HTTP request, funneling into a single HTTP server (FastAPI route); the server hands the request to the TokenizerManager, then on to the GPU-holding Scheduler. One server funnels many requests into the shared engine.

Note the offline Engine walks the same chain's back half: it builds the Engine directly, skipping the leftmost two cells ("HTTP request + FastAPI app"), and the rest is identical. That's exactly why we say server = Engine + an HTTP shell — the server reimplements no runtime logic; it only "answers the call, relays the ticket, replies."

Engine.__init__ launches three processes

Recall Lesson 2's "three-process model": SGLang splits tokenizing, scheduling+forward, detokenizing across processes, talking over ZMQ. And what spins those processes up is Engine.__init__ (the server builds an Engine first, so it goes through this too). Constructing an Engine is igniting the whole runtime:

1

Parse ServerArgs

Collect kwargs like model_path into a ServerArgs deciding parallelism, memory, backend — every config.

2

Launch subprocesses

Call _launch_subprocesses(): build TokenizerManager in the main process, and start one subprocess each for Scheduler and DetokenizerManager.

3

Wire up ZMQ

Create the sockets for inter-process communication, stringing the main process and the two subprocesses into one pipeline.

4

Ready to serve

All three processes in place; the moment generate() is called, a request runs down the chain.

python/sglang/srt/entrypoints/engine.py ::Enginebuilding an Engine = igniting the whole runtime
class Engine(EngineScoreMixin, EngineBase):
    # Entry point to the inference engine, made of three components:
    # 1. TokenizerManager  tokenizes and sends requests to the scheduler (main proc)
    # 2. Scheduler  subprocess: receives, batches, forwards, sends tokens to detokenizer
    # 3. DetokenizerManager  subprocess: detokenizes and sends results back

    def __init__(self, **kwargs):
        server_args = self.server_args_class(**kwargs)   # parse config
        # launch subprocesses: TokenizerManager (main) + Scheduler/Detokenizer (subprocs)
        (tokenizer_manager, template_manager, port_args,
         scheduler_init_result, subprocess_watchdog) = self._launch_subprocesses(
            server_args=server_args, ...)
        self.tokenizer_manager = tokenizer_manager       # generate() goes through it

When to use which

The choice is intuitive: do you need to cross processes/network? If your code is in the same Python process and wants the lowest overhead — running an eval set in batch, generating offline data, or letting an RL trainer roll out in-process (Lesson 51) — use the offline Engine. If you want to serve the model as a service to many concurrent clients, or clients in another language/machine, use the online server and let its HTTP and OpenAI-compat layer catch the whole ecosystem. Remember: their performance core is identical; the only difference is whether you want that HTTP layer.

📌 Key points
  • Two doors: offline Engine (pure in-process Python API, no HTTP) and online server (a FastAPI app from launch_server), both inheriting EngineBase.
  • Offline Engine: sgl.Engine(model_path=...).generate(prompts, sampling_params), lowest overhead, best for batch, eval, RL rollout (trainer calls in-process, Lesson 51).
  • Online server: python -m sglang.launch_server exposes native POST /generate plus OpenAI-compatible routes (/v1/chat/completions, Lesson 15); best for production/multi-client/cross-language.
  • One runtime: the server rewrites no logic — just an HTTP shell over the Engine; from Lesson 14 on, TokenizerManager (Lesson 14), Scheduler (Lesson 18), Detokenizer are shared by both.
  • Ignition: Engine.__init__ parses ServerArgs and _launch_subprocesses spins up the three processes (Lesson 2's three-process model).

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. Which statement about the offline Engine and the online server is most accurate?
  1. Their underlying runtimes differ, so you must learn each separately
  2. The offline Engine also uses HTTP, just on a non-exposed port
  3. The online server reimplements its own scheduling and forward logic, unrelated to the Engine
  4. The online server uses launch_server to start a FastAPI app that wraps an Engine and exposes HTTP routes; the offline Engine is a pure in-process Python API calling generate without HTTP. Both share one runtime; the server reimplements no runtime logic
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. server = Engine + an HTTP shell. launch_server builds an Engine internally, then wraps it with FastAPI/uvicorn exposing native POST /generate and OpenAI-compatible routes; the offline Engine takes the back half of the chain, skipping HTTP. The underlying runtime (TokenizerManager, Scheduler, Detokenizer) is identical, so everything from Lesson 14 on is shared — learn it once.
2. You need rollout in RL training (the trainer generates samples frequently). Why usually prefer the offline Engine over standing up an HTTP server?
  1. Because only the Engine supports sampling params
  2. Because the offline Engine is an in-process direct call with no HTTP parsing, serialization, or network round-trip — lowest latency; the trainer can generate in the same process, ideal for high-frequency rollout
  3. Because the offline Engine produces higher-quality, different-precision outputs
  4. Because an HTTP server cannot load large models
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. RL rollout calls are extremely frequent, so every extra HTTP+serialization+network round-trip is magnified into real overhead. The offline Engine returns Python results in the same process, dropping those layers for lowest latency; the trainer can hold the Engine object directly for rollout (Lesson 51). Output quality and sampling are identical — the only difference is whether you want that HTTP layer.
3. When you construct sgl.Engine(model_path=...), what does Engine.__init__ mainly do?
  1. Parse ServerArgs, then call _launch_subprocesses to spin up three processes: TokenizerManager in the main process plus Scheduler and DetokenizerManager subprocesses, and wire up ZMQ (Lesson 2's three-process model)
  2. Merely load model weights into CPU memory, creating no processes
  3. Do nothing, initializing only on the first generate
  4. Start a FastAPI server and listen on a port
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. Constructing the Engine ignites the whole runtime: gather kwargs into ServerArgs, then _launch_subprocesses builds TokenizerManager in the main process, starts one subprocess each for Scheduler and Detokenizer, and wires ZMQ. Note it does not start FastAPI — the HTTP layer is the server's job. The online server also builds an Engine first, so it goes through this too (Lesson 2).
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • Using the 'one kitchen, two ways to order' analogy, explain to a colleague the relationship between the offline Engine and the online server. State clearly: does the server reimplement the runtime? Are the TokenizerManager, Scheduler, and Detokenizer (from Lesson 14 on) the same for both? And why does that mean you 'learn the internals only once'?
  • Two scenarios: (a) batch-score ten thousand prompts for offline eval; (b) serve a public-facing, multi-language chat product. For each, pick the offline Engine or the online server, and justify your choice from overhead, concurrency, cross-language/cross-machine access, and the OpenAI-compatible ecosystem.

TokenizerManager

Last lesson said: whether you enter through the offline Engine or the online server, requests land on one shared runtime. So once a request is inside, which component catches it first? That is today's star — the TokenizerManager. It is the runtime's front door: it runs in the main process (alongside the HTTP server / Engine), not in the GPU-hungry subprocess. Every request is first tokenized, packed and registered here before being sent on to the scheduler.

Why give it its own lesson? Because the TokenizerManager is the translator between the outside text world and the inner token world, and the async concurrency hub. You send a string; the engine speaks token ids. Hundreds of requests pour in at once — whose reply goes back to whom is its job to coordinate. Understanding this front door is what lets Lesson 16's io_struct messages, Lesson 17's detokenize/streaming, and Lesson 18's scheduler chain into one coherent flow.

🔌 Analogy
Think of the TokenizerManager as a hotel front-desk receptionist. A guest (request) walks up and states their need in spoken words (raw text). The receptionist translates it into a standardized ticket — fills in the room type and requests (sampling params), staples on a booking number (rid), and sends the ticket to the back office (the scheduler subprocess) to actually arrange things. The guest never enters the kitchen; they just wait to be called. Meanwhile the receptionist juggles dozens of guests at once, handing each reply back to exactly the right person. The desk never cooks (never touches the GPU): it only translates, registers, forwards, awaits, and delivers — exactly the TokenizerManager's job.
🌍 Big picture
The TokenizerManager lives in the main process as the CPU-side async coordinator. Its core method generate_request is an async coroutine: it tokenizes the text into token ids (pure CPU string work), builds the sampling params, packs everything into a TokenizedGenerateReqInput (Lesson 16) carrying a unique rid, and sends it over ZMQ to the scheduler subprocess. It then registers a per-request async state keyed by rid and awaits the outputs streaming back from the detokenizer (Lesson 17), yielding them to the caller (HTTP SSE or the Engine). Isolating tokenize/detokenize from the GPU loop in separate processes is what lets CPU and GPU overlap (the zero-overhead idea, Lesson 21).
Fig 1 · Tokenize: text → token ids — the string "SGLang 很快" is first split into the four subwords ["SG","Lang","很","快"], then each is looked up in the vocab and mapped to an integer token id [12, 8801, 332, 1567] (ids illustrative). From here inward, the engine only speaks these numbers.

A concrete example for the granularity of tokenization: the English sentence "Hello, world!" tokenizes into about 4 tokens (["Hello", ",", " world", "!"]), while the Chinese "很快" is usually 2 tokens. How many tokens a piece of text becomes depends on the model's own vocab and BPE merge rules — exactly the first thing the TokenizerManager computes for you at the front door.

Which process? Why is it CPU work

Recall Lesson 2's three-process model: tokenize in the main process, schedule+forward in the scheduler subprocess, detokenize in the detokenizer subprocess. The TokenizerManager sits in the main process slot — same process as the HTTP server / Engine, not in the GPU subprocess. This is a deliberate process-boundary design, not an accident.

The key: tokenize and detokenize are CPU-bound Python string work — table lookups, BPE merges, special-token handling, all CPU, nothing to do with GPU matmuls. If you crammed them into the GPU subprocess, the GPU would idle while the CPU tokenizes and vice versa — the two would serialize and drag each other down. By locking the GPU loop in its own process and letting the TokenizerManager do the CPU-side preprocessing in the main process, the two can truly overlap: while the GPU forwards the previous batch, the CPU is already tokenizing the next. This is the foundation of SGLang's zero-overhead scheduling (Lesson 21), and the TokenizerManager ↔ scheduler hop is exactly the ZMQ/IPC seam.

Main process · TokenizerManager

CPU-side front door: tokenize, build sampling params, pack a TokenizedGenerateReqInput, register async state by rid, await replies. Never touches the GPU — pure Python strings and coroutine scheduling.

Subprocess · Scheduler + GPU

Given already-tokenized requests, it does continuous batching and forward passes (Lesson 18), burning VRAM and kernels. It only speaks token ids, never sees raw text, focused on keeping the GPU fed.

generate_request: a request's journey

When a request arrives, the async coroutine generate_request pushes it down step by step: normalize the args, tokenize into token ids, build sampling params, pack a TokenizedGenerateReqInput with a rid, send over ZMQ to the scheduler, then await the streamed outputs and hand them back piece by piece. Here is the trunk in five steps:

1

tokenize

_tokenize_one_request feeds text to the tokenizer, yielding input_ids (pure CPU work, deliberately kept off the GPU process).

2

build sampling params

Collect temperature / top_p / max_new_tokens into a SamplingParams, then normalize / verify it.

3

pack + stamp rid

Build a TokenizedGenerateReqInput (Lesson 16) carrying a unique rid — the cross-process "ID card".

4

ZMQ send

_send_one_request dispatches the object over a ZMQ socket to the scheduler subprocess; the main process does not block.

5

register and await

Register an async state in rid_to_state, await the outputs streaming back from the detokenizer, and yield them to the caller.

python/sglang/srt/managers/io_struct.py ::GenerateReqInputthe parsed request struct: text or tokens, sampling params, stream flag
@dataclasses.dataclass
class GenerateReqInput:
    # the parsed request that flows from the entry into the engine
    text = None            # the prompt (or a batch of prompts)
    input_ids = None       # OR pre-tokenized token ids
    sampling_params = None # temperature / top_p / max_new_tokens ...
    stream = False         # stream tokens back as they are produced
    rid = None             # request id (assigned per request)

Note that text and input_ids are mutually exclusive: either pass raw text and let the front door tokenize it, or pass token ids directly to skip tokenization. Either way the TokenizerManager stamps a unique rid, collects the sampling_params, and decides whether to return the output as a stream.

The rid round-trip: how the hub routes replies

The TokenizerManager's cleverest part is its async routing. Hundreds of requests arrive concurrently, each assigned a unique rid; after sending, the main process does not block — it records rid → coroutine state in a map (rid_to_state). Outputs flow back interleaved and out of order — a token for #7 now, one for #3 next — and the TokenizerManager uses the rid to route each chunk back to the exact coroutine that is awaiting it. Without the rid, hundreds of concurrent replies would go to the wrong callers. It also handles abort and, for multimodal, image/audio preprocessing.

TokenizerManager
tokenize + stamp rid, main proc
→
Scheduler
batch + forward (subproc)
→
Detokenizer
tokens → text
→
back to TokenizerManager
route by rid to the coroutine
Fig 2 · TokenizerManager ↔ Scheduler (IPC) — the TokenizerManager in the main process pushes the tokenized request over ZMQ PUSH across the process boundary to the Scheduler subprocess (which alone holds the GPU); results return over PULL. Both ends match replies up purely by rid.
What the TokenizerManager ownsMeaning
tokenizeturn text into token ids (CPU work, kept off the GPU process)
sampling paramsbuild and verify SamplingParams (temperature, top_p, max_new_tokens, ...)
ridstamp each request with a unique id — cross-process identity and reply routing
async routingmaintain rid_to_state, route out-of-order replies to the right awaiting coroutine
aborthandle request cancellation, clean up the pending state
mm preprocessimage/audio preprocessing for multimodal requests

Read the table as a whole: the TokenizerManager is an async hub that does only CPU-side coordination and never touches the GPU. It owns the whole front-door job — text→tokens, stamp, forward, await, deliver — so the GPU subprocess can speak only tokens and just compute. Below is the real skeleton of generate_request: tokenize, register, ZMQ send, then await the replies.

python/sglang/srt/managers/tokenizer_manager.py ::TokenizerManager.generate_requestfront door: tokenize → ZMQ send → await replies
async def generate_request(self, obj, request=None):
    self.auto_create_handle_loop()
    obj.normalize_batch_and_arguments()
    self._init_req_state(obj, request)        # register async state by rid

    if obj.is_single:
        # tokenize (CPU work, kept off the GPU process)
        tokenized_obj = await self._tokenize_one_request(obj)
        state = self.rid_to_state[obj.rid]    # find this request's state by rid
        self._send_one_request(tokenized_obj) # send over ZMQ to the scheduler subproc
        async for response in self._wait_one_response(obj, request):
            yield response                    # await streamed outputs, hand back to caller
📌 Key points
  • Front door / main process: the TokenizerManager is the runtime's front door, running in the main process (with the HTTP server / Engine), not in the GPU subprocess.
  • Five-step trunk: the generate_request coroutine — tokenize → build sampling params → pack a TokenizedGenerateReqInput with a rid (Lesson 16) → send over ZMQ to the scheduler → await replies and yield them.
  • What the rid is for: the unique request id is the cross-process ID card; with hundreds of concurrent, out-of-order replies, rid_to_state routes each back to the right awaiting coroutine (streaming, Lesson 17).
  • Why tokenize on CPU: tokenize/detokenize are CPU-bound string work; keeping them off the GPU process lets CPU and GPU overlap (zero-overhead, Lesson 21).
  • Also owns: request abort and multimodal image/audio preprocessing; it is the async concurrency hub and itself never touches the GPU.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. Which process does the TokenizerManager run in, and why?
  1. In the main process (with the HTTP server / Engine), not the GPU subprocess — because tokenize/detokenize are CPU-bound string work, and keeping them off the GPU process lets CPU and GPU overlap (zero-overhead, Lesson 21)
  2. In the GPU subprocess, doing forward passes alongside the Scheduler
  3. It has no process of its own — just a function inside the Scheduler
  4. A fresh process per request, destroyed after use
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. The TokenizerManager is the runtime's front door, doing CPU-side preprocessing in the main process and never touching the GPU. With the GPU loop locked in its own subprocess, the CPU tokenizes the next batch while the GPU forwards the previous one — true overlap, the foundation of Lesson 21's zero-overhead scheduling. The hop to the scheduler is the ZMQ/IPC seam.
2. With hundreds of concurrent requests, what is the most critical role of the rid (request id)?
  1. A cross-process 'ID card': when outputs flow back interleaved and out of order, the TokenizerManager uses the rid in rid_to_state to find the awaiting coroutine and route each chunk back to the right caller
  2. It sets the request's sampling temperature and top_p
  3. It prioritizes requests — smaller numbers compute first
  4. It is an alias for token ids, generated after tokenization
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. After sending, the main process does not block — it records rid → coroutine state in rid_to_state. As replies stream back interleaved, only the unique rid can match each chunk to the correct awaiting coroutine (streaming, Lesson 17). Without it, hundreds of concurrent replies would go to the wrong callers.
3. Inside generate_request, what exactly does the TokenizerManager send over ZMQ to the scheduler subprocess?
  1. An HTTP request object for the scheduler to parse
  2. Model weights and KV-cache pointers
  3. A tokenized TokenizedGenerateReqInput with a unique rid (token ids + sampling params, Lesson 16) — the scheduler only speaks tokens and never sees raw text
  4. The raw prompt text string, leaving the scheduler to tokenize
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. The TokenizerManager first runs _tokenize_one_request to get token ids, builds and verifies SamplingParams, then packs a TokenizedGenerateReqInput with a rid (Lesson 16's io_struct message) and dispatches it via _send_one_request over a ZMQ socket. The text→token translation is done in the main process, so the GPU subprocess only faces tokens and just computes.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • Using the 'hotel front-desk receptionist' analogy, explain the TokenizerManager's job to a colleague. Make clear: what it translates spoken needs into (token ids + params + a booking number rid), why it never 'enters the kitchen' (never touches the GPU), and how it juggles dozens of guests without mixing up replies.
  • Suppose someone proposes moving tokenization into the GPU subprocess to drop the ZMQ hop between the TokenizerManager and the scheduler. Argue what would be lost, from the angles of CPU/GPU overlap (Lesson 21), the GIL, and the main-process vs subprocess separation of duties.

OpenAI/Anthropic/Ollama compat

Last lesson we saw the runtime's front door — the TokenizerManager. But one question it can't answer: out there are clients speaking the OpenAI dialect, the Anthropic dialect, the Ollama dialect — how can they plug into one runtime without changing a line? The answer is today's star — the compat layer. It makes the SGLang server speak several protocols at once, so existing clients work unchanged.

Why a whole lesson on the compat layer? Because it is the seam that lets SGLang drop into the existing ecosystem. You don't rewrite an SDK for SGLang, nor make your team learn a new API — just point an OpenAI client's base_url at your SGLang deployment and it runs immediately. LangChain, the openai Python package, any tool built around the OpenAI API — all plug in seamlessly. This thin adapter layer is the master key by which a private deployment takes over the whole ecosystem.

🔌 Analogy
Picture the compat layer as a row of multilingual translators standing at the door. Guests each speak their own dialect: some OpenAI, some Anthropic, some Ollama. The translators convert each dialect into the one house language the kitchen understands — the native GenerateReqInput (Lesson 16). The kitchen (the runtime) has no idea which dialect a guest originally spoke; it just cooks from that standard ticket. When the dish is ready, the translators translate the same dish back into each guest's dialect and serve it. Guests think "this place speaks my language!" — but there is only one kitchen and one house language; only the translators at the door are multilingual.
🌍 Big picture
The compat layer lives under entrypoints/openai/ and friends, built from a set of serving classes. Take OpenAI: /v1/chat/completions is handled by OpenAIServingChat, /v1/completions by OpenAIServingCompletion, plus /v1/embeddings and more. Every serving class does the same job: translate an OpenAI-shaped request — apply the chat template, build sampling params — into a native GenerateReqInput (Lesson 16) handed to the TokenizerManager (Lesson 14); then map the runtime's outputs back into OpenAI response objects or SSE chunks (chat.completion.chunk). The Anthropic (/v1/messages) and Ollama adapters do the same for their own schemas.

A request's protocol journey

Walk the whole path in your head first. A request's journey is: client SDK → protocol adapter → native request → the same runtime → adapter formats the response. Note the middle stretch — "the same runtime" — whichever dialect the entry spoke, it converges here onto one shared fast path. The adapter only translates at the outermost layer; it never reimplements the engine.

That is the essence of the compat-layer design: the runtime itself is protocol-agnostic — it only knows GenerateReqInput. The adapter does just two things — schema translation (map OpenAI/Anthropic/Ollama fields onto native fields) and chat templating (assemble the messages list into a single prompt per the model's template). Because its job is so narrow, adding a protocol is cheap: write one new adapter. The hottest inference fast path stays shared and optimized once. If every new protocol meant rewriting the engine, that would be an engineering disaster — the compat layer sidesteps it elegantly with "thin adapters + shared core."

ClientOpenAI SDK / LangChain / curl
Speaks the OpenAI dialect: posts /v1/chat/completions with a messages list. Just point base_url at SGLang — no code change.
AdapterProtocol layer OpenAI / Anthropic / Ollama
The serving class does schema translation + chat templating, shaping the dialect request into native form. New protocol = one more adapter.
House languageGenerateReqInput (Lesson 16)
The only native request the runtime understands: token/text + sampling params + rid. Protocol-agnostic; three dialects converge into one here.
Front doorTokenizerManager (Lesson 14)
Takes the native request, tokenizes, stamps a rid, ships it to the scheduler via ZMQ. The shared fast path starts here, identical for all protocols.
Fig 1 · Three protocols → one internal request — OpenAI, Anthropic and Ollama each take their own adapter; after schema translation and chat templating they all converge into one GenerateReqInput. The engine core sees only this unified form and can no longer tell which dialect the request originally spoke.

Route → serving class → native mapping

In code, each HTTP route is bound to a serving class that does the translating. The table below aligns "the route the world sees" with "the class that takes it inside" — at a glance you see the compat layer's division-of-labor map: different dialects take different adapters, but every arrow ends at the same GenerateReqInput.

HTTP routeServing classNative form produced
/v1/chat/completionsOpenAIServingChatapply chat template → GenerateReqInput
/v1/completionsOpenAIServingCompletionraw prompt → GenerateReqInput
/v1/messagesAnthropic adaptermessages → GenerateReqInput
/api/generateOllama adapterOllama fields → GenerateReqInput

A consistent pattern is worth naming: routes differ in shape, the destination is identical. OpenAIServingChat must "apply the chat template" first, because the chat endpoint gives a list of role messages that must be assembled into a single prompt per the model's own template; /v1/completions gets prompt text directly, so its adapter skips templating. Anthropic's /v1/messages names fields differently (system kept separate, max_tokens required), and Ollama's /api/generate is yet another shape — but every adapter emits the same GenerateReqInput. That is "many dialects, one core" at the code level.

Native /generate vs /v1/chat/completions

To really feel where the "translation" happens, put the native interface and the OpenAI-compatible interface side by side. They ultimately drive the same runtime; the difference is only what the request/response envelope looks like — native is SGLang's own shape, OpenAI is a shape mimicked for ecosystem compatibility.

Native POST /generate

SGLang's own shape: the body gives text or input_ids plus sampling_params, and the response is native SGLang JSON. No chat template, fields closest to the core — the native HTTP entry from Lesson 13, ideal for custom clients and the front-end RuntimeEndpoint.

/v1/chat/completions

OpenAI shape: the body is a messages role list that OpenAIServingChat runs through the chat template and translates into GenerateReqInput; the response is mapped to a chat.completion object or a chat.completion.chunk SSE stream (Lesson 17). Any OpenAI client works out of the box.

Fig 2 · Applying the chat template — OpenAI gives a list of {role, content} messages (system / user / assistant); the adapter assembles them per the model's chat template (e.g. <|im_start|>role…<|im_end|>) into a single prompt string, then tokenizes it — exactly the extra step the chat endpoint has over native /generate.

A concrete example. OpenAI's messages is just a list of role-tagged objects, e.g. [{"role":"system","content":"You are helpful"}, {"role":"user","content":"hi"}]; after applying the chat template it becomes one marked-up plain-text string, e.g. <|im_start|>system You are helpful<|im_end|><|im_start|>user hi<|im_end|><|im_start|>assistant, and that string is finally tokenized into tokens for the runtime. Native /generate takes prompt text directly, skipping exactly this step.

python/sglang/srt/entrypoints/openai/serving_chat.py ::OpenAIServingChatadapts an OpenAI chat request into SGLang's internal request
class OpenAIServingChat(OpenAIServingBase):
    # adapts OpenAI /v1/chat/completions to SGLang's internal request
    def _convert_to_internal_request(self, request):
        # 1) apply the chat template to `messages` -> one prompt string
        # 2) map OpenAI params (temperature, max_tokens, ...) -> sampling_params
        # 3) return a GenerateReqInput the engine understands
        ...

Two "OpenAI directions": don't conflate A and B

Here we must nail down a very easy to confuse point that directly bears on understanding both Lesson 12 and this one. "OpenAI" appears twice in SGLang, but they are two completely independent, opposite-facing things:

Two independent OpenAI directions, opposite-facing — never conflate them
Direction A (Lesson 12)SGLang frontend program→calls OUT to OpenAI as backendyour program is the client
Direction B (this lesson)OpenAI client→calls IN to the SGLang serveryour deployment is the OpenAI endpoint

Spell them out. Direction A (Lesson 12): a frontend program you wrote in the SGLang DSL calls OpenAI as a backend — here your program is the client and OpenAI produces tokens remotely. Direction B (this lesson): an OpenAI client (someone's openai SDK, LangChain) calls your SGLang server in turn — here your SGLang deployment masquerades as an OpenAI endpoint, and they plug in by just changing base_url. One is "SGLang calling OpenAI," the other "OpenAI calling SGLang" — opposite directions. This lesson is always Direction B: put an OpenAI mask on your deployment so the whole ecosystem of tools plugs in. Keep these two straight and you'll never get lost over "who is the client and who is the server."

Below is the compat layer's real skeleton — the OpenAIServingChat class, and how it translates an OpenAI-shaped ChatCompletionRequest into a native GenerateReqInput and hands it to tokenizer_manager.generate_request:

python/sglang/srt/entrypoints/openai/serving_chat.py ::OpenAIServingChatOpenAI shape → native GenerateReqInput → front door
class OpenAIServingChat(OpenAIServingBase):
    # Handler for /v1/chat/completions requests

    def _convert_to_internal_request(self, request, raw_request=None):
        # Apply the chat template, assembling messages into a single prompt
        processed_messages = self._process_messages(request, is_multimodal)
        sampling_params = request.to_sampling_params(...)  # OpenAI params → sampling params
        # Build the only "house language" the runtime knows: native GenerateReqInput (Lesson 16)
        adapted_request = GenerateReqInput(
            **prompt_kwargs, sampling_params=sampling_params,
            stream=request.stream, rid=request.rid, ...)
        return adapted_request, request

    async def _generate_chat_stream(self, adapted_request, request, raw_request):
        # Hand to the TokenizerManager (Lesson 14) — the SAME fast path as the native entry
        async for content in self.tokenizer_manager.generate_request(
                adapted_request, raw_request):
            yield content        # then map outputs back to chat.completion.chunk (Lesson 17)
📌 Key points
  • Compat layer = translators: the SGLang server speaks several protocols so existing clients work unchanged; it only does schema translation + chat templating, not a new engine.
  • Request path: client SDK → protocol adapter → native GenerateReqInput (Lesson 16) → the same runtime (TokenizerManager, Lesson 14) → adapter formats the response (SSE, Lesson 17).
  • Route map: /v1/chat/completions→OpenAIServingChat, /v1/completions→OpenAIServingCompletion, /v1/messages→Anthropic, /api/generate→Ollama — all ending at GenerateReqInput.
  • Two OpenAI directions: A (Lesson 12) is an SGLang program calling OUT to OpenAI as backend; B (this lesson) is an OpenAI client calling IN to the SGLang server. Opposite — don't conflate.
  • Why thin adapters: the runtime is protocol-agnostic, adding a protocol is cheap, and the hottest inference fast path is always shared and optimized once.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. What does the compat layer (OpenAI/Anthropic/Ollama) actually do so existing clients work against SGLang unchanged?
  1. It forwards SGLang requests to the real OpenAI servers to compute
  2. It runs a separate inference engine and KV cache per protocol
  3. It requires clients to switch to a SGLang-only SDK
  4. A set of serving classes that only do schema translation + chat templating: shape the dialect request into a native GenerateReqInput (Lesson 16) for the TokenizerManager (Lesson 14), then map outputs back to that protocol's response/SSE — not a reimplemented engine
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. The runtime is protocol-agnostic and only knows GenerateReqInput. The adapter does two narrow things at the outermost layer: map OpenAI/Anthropic/Ollama fields to native fields (schema translation) and assemble messages into a prompt per the model's template (chat templating). Because the job is narrow, adding a protocol is just one more adapter, and the hottest fast path stays shared and optimized once.
2. "OpenAI" has two directions in SGLang. Which one does this lesson (Direction B) cover?
  1. An OpenAI client (someone's openai SDK / LangChain) calls IN to your SGLang server — your deployment masquerades as an OpenAI endpoint, and they plug in by just changing base_url
  2. Your SGLang frontend program calls OUT to OpenAI as a backend (that is Direction A, Lesson 12)
  3. The two directions are the same thing and need not be distinguished
  4. SGLang proxies both request and response to the real OpenAI
Show answer & explanation click to expand
