Answer: A. Direction A (Lesson 12): the SGLang program is the client, calling OpenAI as a backend. Direction B (this lesson): the OpenAI client is the caller and your SGLang server is the callee, wearing an OpenAI mask so the whole ecosystem plugs in. One is 'SGLang calling OpenAI,' the other 'OpenAI calling SGLang' — opposite, never conflate.
3. Why does any OpenAI client work against the SGLang server out of the box?
  1. Because SGLang implements OpenAI's HTTP protocol: /v1/chat/completions is handled by OpenAIServingChat, which translates the OpenAI-shaped request into a native one, runs the same runtime, and maps results back to chat.completion objects/SSE chunks — so the client only points base_url at SGLang
  2. Because the two protocols' fields are identical, needing no translation
  3. Because SGLang secretly calls the real OpenAI API under the hood
  4. Because OpenAI clients auto-detect and switch to SGLang's native protocol
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. An OpenAI client only sends requests and parses responses per OpenAI's HTTP contract. SGLang's compat layer under entrypoints/openai/ implements that contract: each route binds a serving class that translates, the request becomes a native GenerateReqInput on the shared fast path, and the response is formatted back into OpenAI objects or stream chunks. The client never notices SGLang underneath — just change base_url.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • Using the 'row of multilingual translators at the door' analogy, explain the compat layer to a colleague. Make clear: guests speak the OpenAI/Anthropic/Ollama dialects, the translators convert each into the one house language the kitchen understands (native GenerateReqInput), there is only one kitchen with a shared cooking path, and the dish is translated back into each dialect. Explain why this is 'thin adapters + shared core' rather than a separate engine per protocol.
  • Distinguish and compare the two 'OpenAI directions': in Direction A (Lesson 12) the SGLang frontend program calls OUT to OpenAI as a backend — who is the client? In Direction B (this lesson) an OpenAI client calls IN to the SGLang server — who is the server? Then describe how, in Direction B, a /v1/chat/completions request flows through OpenAIServingChat to apply the chat template, build a GenerateReqInput, hand it to the TokenizerManager, and finally map back to SSE.

IO structs & IPC

Lesson 14 showed the front door (TokenizerManager) and Lesson 15 the multi-protocol translators, but one thing kept slipping by: these three processes do not share memory — so what exactly travels between them? That is today's star: the group of message structs in io_struct.py. Processes cannot read each other's variables; they can only pass messages. And io_struct.py pins down the shape of those messages as Python dataclasses, serialized over ZMQ.

Why a whole lesson for it? Because this set of structs is effectively the runtime's "type system" and wire protocol. Every later component — the scheduler (Lesson 18), the worker, the detokenizer (Lesson 17) — talks to the others in these structs. Grab the single thread of "how a request changes shape across processes," and those seemingly complex components string together like beads. Skip it, and the scheduler source will drown you in TokenizedGenerateReqInput and BatchTokenIDOutput — they are not details, they are the skeleton.

There is a deeper angle too: making communication explicit as data structures is itself an engineering discipline. Once the process boundary is pinned by dataclasses, "who sends which field, who fills it, who reads it" becomes black and white — printable, breakpoint-able, unit-testable. Compared with implicit shared state, this "everything is a message" design costs a few extra classes but buys debuggability and evolvability — a lesson distributed systems keep re-teaching.

🔌 Analogy
Think of these structs as standardized forms and envelopes moving between departments. For one case, each department turns it into a new official form: the front desk takes an intake form (raw ask: text, sampling prefs, stream flag); tokenization turns it into a work order (token ids + params, sent to the floor); the floor produces a results sheet (a batch of token ids); finally it is typed up as a printed letter (decoded text) and mailed back. Every form is stamped with the same case number (rid) in the corner, so even with dozens of cases interleaving on the conveyor belt, nothing is misrouted. Departments never share a drawer (no shared memory) — they cooperate purely by handing forms across (passing messages).
🌍 Big picture
io_struct.py lives under python/sglang/srt/managers/ and is a pure message-definition file: almost no algorithms, just @dataclass after @dataclass. They line up along the request's lifecycle — GenerateReqInput (the user-facing request the front door receives) → TokenizedGenerateReqInput (post-tokenization, the one that actually crosses ZMQ to the scheduler) → BatchTokenIDOutput (batched token ids from scheduler/worker) → BatchStrOutput (text decoded by the detokenizer). Each struct marks who produces and who consumes it. They travel over ZMQ IPC sockets (a port per process), and the rid routes interleaved batched outputs back to the right request. There are also standalone control messages: abort, flush_cache, health.
Figure 1 · The IPC message types — the three processes share no memory and cooperate only by passing dataclasses: a GenerateReqInput becomes a TokenizedGenerateReqInput sent to the scheduler, which emits a BatchTokenIDOutput to the detokenizer, whose BatchStrOutput is routed back to the client via the front door.

Why message passing, not shared memory

Recall the three-process model (Lesson 2): the main process (TokenizerManager), the scheduler subprocess, the detokenizer subprocess. They are three separate OS processes, each with its own memory — an object in the main process is invisible and untouchable to the scheduler. This is deliberate: splitting processes sidesteps Python's GIL and lets CPU and GPU truly overlap (Lesson 21). The price: they can no longer cooperate by "reading the same variable" — only by handing messages back and forth.

So the question becomes: what do messages look like, and how do they cross the wire? SGLang's answer is plain and effective — define shape with dataclasses, do transport with ZMQ. Dataclasses make every field obvious and printable; ZMQ provides lightweight inter-process sockets that serialize an object to bytes, send it, and deserialize it on the other side. Together they yield communication that is cheap, explicit, and debuggable — you can literally print a message and see its tokens and sampling params.

Shared memory? No

The three processes each own separate memory; one process's objects are unreadable to another. That is the boundary cost of splitting processes to dodge the GIL and overlap CPU/GPU (Lesson 21).

Message passing? Yes

Cooperation is purely by passing messages: shape with dataclass, cross the wire via ZMQ serialization. Cheap, explicit, printable and breakpoint-able — the classic distributed choice.

A request's lifecycle: a relay of message shapes

What brings this design alive is following one request and watching it swap structs at each process boundary. It is a tight relay: each leg's "output message" is exactly the next leg's "input message," and the baton carried throughout is the rid.

GenerateReqInput
user request at the door: text+params+stream
→
TokenizedGenerateReqInput
post-tokenize, sent via ZMQ to scheduler
→
BatchTokenIDOutput
batched token ids from scheduler/worker
→
BatchStrOutput
text decoded by the detokenizer
→
route by rid to client
hand back to the awaiting request
Figure 2 · One round-trip — the request is pickle-serialized and travels right through the three processes over zmq sockets, then the response returns the same way, forming a loop. Note: what crosses between processes are plain Python dataclasses (serialized over IPC), not HTTP between processes — HTTP exists only at the outermost edge facing the client.

A concrete picture: the main process hands the TokenizedGenerateReqInput object to ZMQ, which uses pickle (and msgpack on some channels) to flatten it into bytes, sends it over an IPC socket to the scheduler subprocess, and rebuilds the same-shaped object on the other side — both ends hold the same dataclass, not an HTTP body.

Leg by leg. Leg one: the TokenizerManager (Lesson 14) receives a GenerateReqInput — the user-facing request: raw text, sampling params, a stream flag, maybe images/audio. Note: this object does not cross processes; it lives only in the main process. Leg two: tokenization turns text into token ids and packs a TokenizedGenerateReqInput — this is the message actually serialized by ZMQ and sent across the boundary to the scheduler. In other words, what crosses ZMQ to the scheduler is tokenized ids, not raw text. That is the one line to memorize this lesson.

Leg three: the scheduler (Lesson 18) groups requests into a ScheduleBatch (Lesson 19), runs the forward pass, and emits a BatchTokenIDOutput — note it is batched: one message holds the new token ids for many requests at once, with a rids list marking which segment belongs to whom. Leg four: the detokenizer (Lesson 17) decodes ids back to text as a BatchStrOutput (with output_strs). Final leg: results flow back to the TokenizerManager, which uses the rid to route each piece to the right awaiting coroutine and hands it to the client.

Message structKey fieldsProducer → Consumer
GenerateReqInputtext / input_ids, sampling_params, stream, ridclient/compat → TokenizerManager (no IPC)
TokenizedGenerateReqInputrid, input_ids, sampling_params, streamTokenizerManager →(ZMQ)→ Scheduler
BatchTokenIDOutputrids, decode_ids/output_ids, finished_reasonsScheduler/worker →(ZMQ)→ Detokenizer
BatchStrOutputrids, output_strs, output_ids, finish infoDetokenizer →(ZMQ)→ TokenizerManager → client
python/sglang/srt/managers/io_struct.py ::BatchTokenIDOutputScheduler -> Detokenizer: a step's raw token outputs
@dataclasses.dataclass
class BatchTokenIDOutput(BaseBatchReq):
    # Scheduler -> Detokenizer: one step's raw token outputs to be turned into text
    finished_reasons: list      # None / stop / length, per request
    decoded_texts: list         # text decoded so far (for incremental decode)
    decode_ids: list            # the token ids to detokenize
    read_offsets: list          # where to resume detokenizing each request
    output_ids: list            # the newly produced token ids
    skip_special_tokens: list

Feel the "batched" nature of this edge concretely: in one step the scheduler runs rid=7, rid=3, and rid=9 together, so it packs all three requests' new token ids into a single BatchTokenIDOutput, with the rids list from BaseBatchReq marking which segment is whose; that one message is then sent over ZMQ (serialized with pickle/msgpack) to the detokenizer.

Read the table top-to-bottom and a neat pattern appears: each row's consumer is the next row's producer; the four structs link end-to-end into the full loop from "human text" to "tokens" and back to "human text." Note the switch from singular to plural: inbound is single (GenerateReqInput/TokenizedGenerateReqInput, carrying rid), outbound is batched (Batch*Output, carrying rids) — because the scheduler batches many requests together, so outputs are naturally batched. That is continuous batching (Lesson 18) showing through.

rid: the case number threaded throughout

What keeps these messages from getting scrambled across three processes is one humble field: rid (request id). It is created at the GenerateReqInput, copied into TokenizedGenerateReqInput all the way to the scheduler, and flows back in the rids lists of BatchTokenIDOutput/BatchStrOutput. Because the scheduler batches many requests together, the returning output is interleaved and batched — one message holds tokens for request 7, request 3, request 9. Without rid, the TokenizerManager could not split and route that interleaved batch back to the right awaiting coroutines.

How rid routes interleaved batched outputs
outrid=7: tokrid=3: tokrid=9: tok→one BatchTokenIDOutputbatched, interleaved
splitby rid→7→coro A3→coro B9→coro Ceach to its own

So rid is not optional metadata — it is the root that lets this "everything is a message" system run concurrent yet unscrambled. It is like the tracking number on a parcel: packages get broken apart, co-mingled, and redistributed at the sorting center, yet are tracked precisely and delivered to the right person — all by that number. Understand the round-trip of rid and you understand the foundation shared by SGLang's async routing (Lesson 14) and streaming (Lesson 17). Here is the real skeleton of the core message that actually crosses ZMQ:

python/sglang/srt/managers/io_struct.py ::TokenizedGenerateReqInputthe message that actually crosses ZMQ to the scheduler
@dataclass
class TokenizedGenerateReqInput(BaseReq):   # BaseReq supplies rid (the cross-process id)
    input_text: str                       # raw text (kept for debug/logging)
    input_ids: Optional[array[int]]        # tokenized ids — what actually feeds the model
    mm_inputs: object                      # multimodal inputs (image/audio)
    sampling_params: SamplingParams        # temperature/top_p/max_new_tokens, ...
    return_logprob: bool
    stream: bool                           # whether to stream back (Lesson 17)
    # ...then serialized by ZMQ and sent to the scheduler (Lesson 18)

Notice this struct has no user-level text-list shape; instead it carries the already-tokenized input_ids — confirming the key line: past the ZMQ wire, downstream only knows tokens, never human text. input_text is retained, but only for debugging and logs; what drives the model is input_ids.

📌 Key points
  • No shared memory, only messages: the three processes (Lesson 2) own separate memory and cooperate via @dataclass messages in io_struct.py + ZMQ serialization.
  • Lifecycle = a relay of messages: GenerateReqInput → TokenizedGenerateReqInput → BatchTokenIDOutput → BatchStrOutput, swapping struct at each process.
  • What crosses ZMQ is tokens, not text: the scheduler (Lesson 18) actually receives the tokenized TokenizedGenerateReqInput (with input_ids); GenerateReqInput lives only in the main process.
  • rid threads throughout: the request id routes interleaved batched outputs (the rids list) back to the right awaiting coroutine (async routing Lesson 14, streaming Lesson 17).
  • It is the runtime's "type system": scheduler, worker, and detokenizer all speak these structs; abort/flush_cache/health are standalone control messages.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. Why do SGLang's three processes communicate via io_struct.py dataclasses + ZMQ instead of sharing objects directly?
  1. Because Python has no way to share memory between processes at all
  2. Because the three processes (Lesson 2) own separate memory, so one process's objects are unreadable to another — splitting processes is the necessary cost of dodging the GIL and overlapping CPU/GPU (Lesson 21), so messages must be shaped by dataclasses and serialized over ZMQ, buying cheap, explicit, printable/breakpoint-able cooperation
  3. Because only this lets you call CUDA kernels on the GPU
  4. Because dataclasses run faster than shared memory, purely a performance optimization
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. The point isn't 'speed' but 'boundaries': the three are separate OS processes with mutually invisible memory. Splitting them dodges the GIL and overlaps CPU/GPU; the cost is no shared variables, only passing messages. io_struct.py pins message shapes with dataclasses and ZMQ handles serialized transport, giving an explicit, debuggable cross-process protocol.
2. When a request actually crosses ZMQ to the scheduler (Lesson 18), which struct travels, and what does it carry?
  1. It's GenerateReqInput, carrying the user's raw text string
  2. It's TokenizedGenerateReqInput, carrying already-tokenized ids (input_ids) + sampling params + rid — the raw text was tokenized in the main process, and past the wire downstream only knows tokens, not human text
  3. It's BatchStrOutput, carrying decoded text
  4. The tokenizer object itself is shared directly with the scheduler process
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. GenerateReqInput is the user-facing request that lives only in the main process and does not cross processes. Only after tokenization is it packed into TokenizedGenerateReqInput, which holds input_ids rather than raw text and is the message ZMQ serializes to the scheduler. So what crosses is tokens, not text — reflecting that tokenization stays in the main process, keeping the linguistic burden at the outermost layer (Lesson 14).
3. The scheduler batches many requests together, so the returning BatchTokenIDOutput is interleaved and batched. What does rid do here?
  1. rid is the request id threaded through every message: each token segment in the batched output carries its own rid, so the TokenizerManager splits and routes the interleaved output back to the right awaiting coroutine; without it, hundreds of concurrent replies would be misdelivered
  2. rid is part of the sampling params, controlling temperature and top_p
  3. rid is just an optional logging field; removing it doesn't affect routing
  4. rid decides which GPU computes each request
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. Because outputs are batched and interleaved (one message mixes tokens for requests 7, 3, 9), a stable identifier is needed to place each segment. rid is created at GenerateReqInput, copied into the tokenized struct, and flows back in the rids lists of the Batch*Output — the foundation shared by async routing (Lesson 14) and streaming (Lesson 17).
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • Using the 'standardized forms moving between departments' analogy, describe a request's lifecycle across the three processes: how it goes from GenerateReqInput (intake form) to TokenizedGenerateReqInput (work order, crossing ZMQ to the scheduler), to BatchTokenIDOutput (results sheet), and finally BatchStrOutput (printed letter). Explain in particular why 'in' is singular while 'out' is batched, and what the rid stamped on each form does.
  • Someone argues: 'Just let the three processes share one block of memory and read/write each other's request objects directly — wouldn't skipping serialization be faster?' Rebut this: from the angles of the GIL, CPU/GPU overlap (Lesson 21), fault isolation, and debuggability, explain why SGLang prefers the 'everything is a message' design with dataclasses + ZMQ even at the cost of serialization.

Detokenizer & streaming

Lesson 14 covered the runtime's entrance — the TokenizerManager turns human text into token ids and sends them to the GPU. This lesson covers the exit: how the token ids the GPU emits one at a time become readable text again, then stream back to the client piece by piece, like a typewriter. The component responsible is its own subprocess — the DetokenizerManager. It is the last component in the chain that speaks human language, and it closes Part 4.

Why give it its own process and its own lesson? Because stitching token ids back into text is far more than a table lookup. It solves two genuinely tricky problems: first, incrementality — each step must emit only the small new slice, not re-decode and re-send the whole generated text every time; second, multi-byte boundaries — a token may decode to only half a Chinese character or half an emoji, so it must wait for a complete character before emitting, never feeding a half-character to the screen. Grasp these two and you'll see why streaming looks so smooth while quietly relying on careful offset bookkeeping.

🔌 Analogy
Think of the detokenizer as a simultaneous interpreter. The speaker (GPU) keeps spilling out words; every few seconds the interpreter opens their mouth — but speaks only the words that appeared since they last spoke, never replaying the whole speech (that's sent_offset remembering "how far I've spoken"). Better still: when a syllable is only half out, the interpreter holds back and waits for the full word before voicing it — never a half-syllable (that's the "hold incomplete tokens" handling). So the audience always hears coherent, complete, never-repeated sentences. That's exactly the detokenizer's job: emit only the new, wait until whole, deliver by id.
🌍 Big picture
Recall Lesson 2's three-process model: tokenize in the main process, schedule+forward in the scheduler subprocess, detokenize in the detokenizer subprocess. After each step, the scheduler packs the batch's newly generated token ids into a BatchTokenIDOutput (Lesson 16) and sends it to the detokenizer; the detokenizer decodes ids into text, packs a BatchStrOutput, and sends it back to the TokenizerManager (Lesson 14), which routes it by rid to the awaiting coroutine, finally streaming it to the HTTP client via SSE. Detokenize gets its own process because it is pure CPU string work that can overlap with the GPU forward loop without either waiting on the other (the zero-overhead idea, Lesson 21).

Where it lives: the runtime's last door

The detokenizer is the pipeline's exit gate. It only speaks token ids and never touches the GPU: input is the batches of new tokens from the scheduler, output is slices of readable text. It mirrors the tokenizer exactly — one splits text into numbers (in), the other stitches numbers back into text (out). Note it sends results back to the TokenizerManager, not the client: only the TokenizerManager's rid_to_state map knows "which awaiting coroutine this rid belongs to and how to yield it to the upstream SSE".

Trace the exit path: the scheduler (subproc) finishes its forward and emits this step's new token ids; the DetokenizerManager (subproc) decodes ids into text and does the incremental slice; the slice returns to the main process's TokenizerManager, routed by rid to the awaiting coroutine; and finally it streams piece by piece via SSE to the HTTP client, surfacing as a "typewriter". Four stages, three process boundaries, all stitched into a loop by the rid.

Incremental detokenize: the magic of sent_offset

This is the lesson's most central — and most overlooked — piece. The naive approach: every step, decode all tokens so far into the full text and send the whole thing. It looks simple but has two fatal flaws: first, resending the whole string each step makes traffic grow quadratically (O(n²)) with length; second, the client repeatedly receives duplicate prefixes and must itself figure out "which part is new".

The right approach is incremental: each request keeps an offset sent_offset recording "which character I've sent up to". After decoding the current full text output_str each step, emit only the new slice output_str[sent_offset:], then advance sent_offset to len(output_str). Next step, again emit only the new tail. Every character crosses the wire exactly once; traffic is linear.

1

receive new token ids

The scheduler delivers this step's newly generated token ids (in a BatchTokenIDOutput, Lesson 16), appended to the request's existing id sequence.

2

decode the full text

Decode the current id sequence into the full-text-so-far output_str (with multi-byte boundary handling).

3

slice only the new part

Take incremental = output_str[sent_offset:] — only the small slice that appeared since the last send.

4

advance the offset

sent_offset = len(output_str), recording "sent up to here" so the next step never re-sends already-sent characters.

5

pack as an SSE chunk

The new slice rides a BatchStrOutput back to the TokenizerManager, then streams to the client as one SSE chunk — the "typewriter" effect.

Fig 1 · Incremental detokenize — token ids arrive one by one: [12, 8801] decode into "SG"+"Lang", and the next [332] decodes into "很". A sent_offset remembers "where decoding left off", so each step decodes only the small new piece instead of re-decoding the whole generated sequence. Subtlety: some tokens only complete a character when combined with later bytes — incremental detokenize waits until it is whole before emitting.

A concrete, character-level example: at some step sent_offset = 6, the step decodes output_str = "SGLang很" (length 7), so it takes only output_str[6:] = "很" to emit and advances sent_offset to 7. On the client side, that step is exactly one SSE frame: data: {"text": "很"}.

To make the difference between "resend everything" and "send only the new" obvious, compare them side by side:

❌ Re-decode all each step

Decode all tokens and send the whole text every step. The client keeps receiving duplicate prefixes, traffic grows quadratically (O(n²)) with length — especially wasteful for long outputs — and the burden of "finding the new part" is dumped on the client.

✅ Incremental slice (sent_offset)

Each step sends only the small new slice output_str[sent_offset:] and advances the offset. Every character crosses the wire once, traffic is linear, the client just concatenates, and it maps naturally onto an SSE stream.

Multi-byte boundaries: why hold back

Incremental slicing hides another difficulty: one token is not one character. A Chinese character or an emoji often takes several bytes — sometimes spanning several tokens — to complete. If a step decodes a tail that is an incomplete UTF-8 sequence (half a character, a broken emoji) and sends it as-is, the client flashes a garbled box �. So the detokenizer must hold back bytes that don't yet form a complete character and wait until more tokens arrive to complete it. The table below shows how sent_offset advances per step and stays put when only half a character is available:

sent_offset advancing per step: the highlight is the slice newly emitted this step
Step 1output_str="你好"→emit "你好"sent_offset: 0 → 2
Step 2tail is half a char→hold, emit nothingsent_offset stays: 2
Step 3output_str="你好世界"→emit "世界"sent_offset: 2 → 4
Step 4output_str="你好世界！"→emit "！"sent_offset: 4 → 5

Stop conditions and SSE: when generation ends

Streaming always needs an end. A request's generation halts under one of three conditions, and the detokenizer/scheduler trims the output at the stop boundary accordingly (sampling and stop details in Lesson 28):

Stop conditionMeaningEffect on output
EOS tokenthe model generated the end-of-sequence tokennatural finish, this request ends streaming
stop stringa user-specified stop string is matched (e.g. "\n\n")trim off the stop string and everything after it
max_new_tokensreached this request's max allowed new tokensforce-truncate, emit the final slice and end
Fig 2 · Streaming SSE frames — on a time axis, each generated token becomes one data: frame sent to the client at its moment t_N; so the user watches the text appear progressively instead of waiting for the whole answer to finish before getting it all at once.

When generation ends, the detokenizer materializes the request's full text, calls trim_matched_stop to cut off the stop string, emits that last tail (again only output_str[sent_offset:]), and then deletes the request's decode state. Each new slice flows back via the TokenizerManager (which yields chunks per request), and the HTTP server route (http_server.py's generate_request) wraps them with StreamingResponse / media_type="text/event-stream", pushing each chunk as one Server-Sent Event to the browser (the offline Engine path has no SSE) — that's the typewriter experience of text "popping out one bit at a time". Below is the real skeleton of the detokenizer's incremental logic:

python/sglang/srt/managers/detokenizer_manager.py ::DetokenizerManagerincremental slice: output_str[sent_offset:] + advance offset
# each request's decode state carries a sent_offset
class DecodeStatus:
    decoded_text: str
    sent_offset: int = 0   # character position already sent

# finished: materialize full text, trim the stop, emit the tail
output_str = self.trim_matched_stop(
    s.get_decoded_text() + new_text,
    recv_obj.finished_reasons[i],
    recv_obj.no_stop_trim[i],
)
incremental_output = output_str[s.sent_offset :]  # slice only the new part
s.sent_offset = len(output_str)                   # advance the offset
output_strs.append(incremental_output)
python/sglang/srt/managers/detokenizer_manager.py ::DetokenizerManagerincrementally decode token ids into text and stream them back
class DetokenizerManager:
    # turns token ids back into text and streams deltas to the client
    def event_loop(self):
        # receive BatchTokenIDOutput from the Scheduler, detokenize
        # incrementally, emit BatchStrOutput
        ...
    def handle_batch_token_id_out(self, recv_obj):
        ...   # decode only the NEW text since each request's read_offset
    def trim_matched_stop(self, ...):
        ...   # cut the text at a matched stop sequence
📌 Key points
  • Exit / own subprocess: the DetokenizerManager is the runtime's exit, running in its own subprocess (Lesson 2's three-process model); it only speaks token ids and never touches the GPU.
  • Message round-trip: the scheduler sends BatchTokenIDOutput (new token ids); the detokenizer decodes and returns BatchStrOutput (Lesson 16) to the TokenizerManager (Lesson 14), routed back by rid.
  • Incremental is key: each request keeps a sent_offset; each step emits only output_str[sent_offset:] then advances the offset — avoiding the O(n²) and duplicate prefixes of full resends.
  • Multi-byte boundaries: incomplete UTF-8 (half a Chinese character / broken emoji) must be held back until a full character forms — never stream half a character.
  • Stop and SSE: generation ends on EOS / stop string / max_new_tokens (Lesson 28); the TokenizerManager yields slices and the HTTP server route wraps them into an SSE "typewriter" via StreamingResponse (the offline Engine path has no SSE). A separate process so it can overlap the GPU (zero-overhead, Lesson 21).

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. Each step, the detokenizer decodes all tokens so far into the full text and sends the whole thing to the client. What's the fundamental problem, and what's the right approach?
  1. No problem at all — full resends are simplest and fastest, no optimization needed
  2. The problem is too few token ids; you should wait until all are generated and send once
  3. Resending everything makes traffic grow quadratically (O(n²)) with length, and the client keeps receiving duplicate prefixes; the right approach is incremental: each request keeps a sent_offset and each step emits only the new slice output_str[sent_offset:] then advances the offset, so every character crosses the wire once
  4. The problem is decoding is too slow; detokenize should be moved to the GPU
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. Naive full resends have two fatal flaws: O(n²) traffic and duplicate prefixes that dump the 'find the new part' burden on the client. Incremental slicing uses sent_offset to track 'how far sent', emitting only output_str[sent_offset:] each step then advancing — linear traffic, the client just concatenates, and it fits SSE naturally.
2. After a step, the tail of the text is exactly half a Chinese character (an incomplete UTF-8 sequence). What should the detokenizer do, and why?
  1. Discard the half-character and keep decoding later tokens
  2. Hold the half-character back, emit nothing, and don't advance sent_offset yet — wait for more tokens next step to complete the character before emitting; otherwise the client flashes a garbled box �, streaming half a character
  3. Immediately terminate this request's generation and raise a UTF-8 error
  4. Just send the half-character; the client will auto-repair the garbling
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. One token ≠ one character: Chinese characters and emojis often span multiple bytes or even tokens. Sending incomplete UTF-8 as-is shows a �. So the detokenizer must hold back bytes that don't yet form a complete character and emit only once whole — exactly why streaming looks smooth and never spits half a character.
3. Why does SGLang put detokenize in its own subprocess rather than doing it inside the scheduler or main process?
  1. Because detokenize needs direct GPU VRAM access, requiring a separate process
  2. Because detokenize is pure CPU string work; isolating it in its own process lets it overlap the GPU forward loop without either waiting (the zero-overhead idea, Lesson 21) — echoing Lesson 2's three-process model: tokenize / schedule+forward / detokenize each in one process
  3. Because subprocesses get higher CPU priority than the main process
  4. Purely a historical accident with no real significance
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. Stitching token ids back into text is CPU-bound string work, unrelated to GPU matmuls. Inside the GPU process, the GPU would idle while the CPU builds strings. As a separate subprocess, detokenize truly overlaps the GPU forward — the foundation of Lesson 21's zero-overhead scheduling and consistent with Lesson 2's three-process boundaries.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • Using the 'simultaneous interpreter speaks only the new words and waits for a whole word' analogy, describe end-to-end how a streaming request goes from the GPU emitting token ids to the client seeing 'typewriter' text. Call out: the BatchTokenIDOutput→BatchStrOutput (Lesson 16) round-trip, why the detokenizer sends results back to the TokenizerManager (Lesson 14) instead of straight to the client, how sent_offset guarantees 'add-only, never-repeat', and why incomplete UTF-8 must be held back.
  • When does generation end? List the three stop conditions (EOS token / stop string / max_new_tokens, see Lesson 28), explain each one's effect on the final output, and explain why on finish the detokenizer calls trim_matched_stop to cut the stop string before emitting the last output_str[sent_offset:]. Finally, explain how the HTTP server route turns the slices TokenizerManager yields into an SSE 'typewriter' via StreamingResponse (text/event-stream) — and why the offline Engine path has no SSE.

The scheduler event loop

Welcome to Part 5 — the engine's heart. The previous four parts showed how a request comes in (HTTP, tokenize, IPC) and goes out (detokenize, streaming). Now the camera turns to the one role in the middle that actually makes decisions: the Scheduler. It runs in its own subprocess, and at its core is an infinite loop called event_loop_normal — the engine's heartbeat. Each beat (we call it one step) runs five things — "receive → enqueue → form batch → forward → process result" — as fast as possible, then immediately starts the next beat. This lesson sits right on that heart and listens to every beat.

🔌 Analogy
Picture the scheduler as an air-traffic controller's radar sweep. Every revolution, the controller repeats the same routine: first take in new arrivals and put them in the holding pattern; then decide which planes land/take off this sweep (the runway is finite — you can't clear them all); then clear those planes and watch them execute; finally handle what just happened — a plane that landed frees the runway, one that departed is closed out. Then the radar sweeps again, forever. There is one tower, it flies no plane itself, yet every sweep's scheduling is its call. That is the scheduler's event loop.
🌍 Big picture
In one line: the scheduler is an infinite loop, in its own subprocess, beating thousands of times per second. Each beat (step) does five things in order — recv_requests (receive), process_input_requests (enqueue), get_next_batch_to_run (form batch), run_batch (forward), process_batch_result (finish). It is the engine's single decision-maker: it owns the KV-cache accounting and decides who is in this step's batch. It only decides, never computes — the real GPU matmuls go to TpWorker→ModelRunner (Lesson 24). The shorter and faster the loop, the higher the throughput — exactly what the overlap scheduler (Lesson 21) later optimizes.

The five actions of a heartbeat: what happens in one step

The event loop is essentially while True. Setting aside the exit and idle branches, each round (one step) is a five-stage pipeline. Memorize the order and you hold the master index to all of Part 5 — every later lesson just zooms into one of these stages:

1

recv_requests() — receive

Drain the ZMQ inbox of all new TokenizedGenerateReqInput (Lesson 16, sent by the TokenizerManager). If none, you get an empty list — it never blocks.

2

process_input_requests() — enqueue

Add new requests to the waiting queue; also handle control messages (abort / flush cache / update weights, etc.).

3

get_next_batch_to_run() — form batch

Build this step's batch: budget permitting, form a prefill batch from waiting reqs, else a decode batch of running reqs (Lessons 19/20). Continuous batching (Lesson 5) re-happens here every step.

4

run_batch() — forward

Hand the batch to TpWorker → ModelRunner.forward on the GPU (Lesson 24), get logits, then sample the new tokens.

5

process_batch_result() — finish

Append new tokens; detect finished reqs (stop conditions); free their KV slots; send outputs to the DetokenizerManager (Lesson 17). Then go back to step 1.

These five steps are very short, yet run thousands of times per second. The key is that it's a closed loop: right after step 5 finishes, it returns to step 1 to receive new requests — there is no notion of "finish this batch entirely before serving anyone new." Because every revolution re-runs step 3 get_next_batch_to_run, the batch is re-formed over and over, finished reqs leave on the spot and waiting ones fill in instantly — this is exactly where Lesson 5's continuous batching physically happens.

The loop as a diagram: how a request flows through the heart

If you draw "how data flows" inside one step, you get this pipeline: requests enter from the inbox, get scheduled into a batch, go forward on the GPU, and the result splits two ways — new tokens go to detokenize, finished reqs are evicted and free their VRAM — then it loops back to the top. Note the loop-back arrow: it is the soul of the "heartbeat," meaning this pipeline never stops.

Inbox
ZMQ recv
recv_requests
→
Schedule
get_next_batch
_to_run (decide)
→
GPU forward
run_batch
forward+sample
→
Finish
append tokens
evict/detok
↺
Next beat
back to inbox
step + 1

In this diagram only the "Schedule" cell is pure-CPU decision, and only the "GPU forward" cell actually burns compute. This split is crucial: while the CPU forms the batch, the GPU is idle; while the GPU does forward, the CPU is idle. The plain event_loop_normal runs them strictly serially — which is why event_loop_overlap (Lesson 21) exists: it is the same loop, but pipelined so the CPU's batching/finishing is hidden behind the previous step's GPU compute, keeping the GPU from ever idling.

Where each step goes: the loop IS Part 5's table of contents

These five steps map almost one-to-one onto the lessons ahead. Treat this table as a navigation map: jump from "which step of the heartbeat" to its detail lesson, and conversely, while reading a detail lesson remember where it sits in the whole step.

StageWhat it doesDetailed in
recv_requestsDrain the ZMQ inbox, receive TokenizedGenerateReqInputLesson 16 io_struct / IPC
process_input_requestsNew reqs into the waiting queue; handle abort/flush control msgsLesson 19 Req / ScheduleBatch
get_next_batch_to_runForm this step's batch: prefill first, else decode (continuous batching)Lesson 20 policy · Lesson 22 chunked prefill
run_batchHand to TpWorker→ModelRunner.forward on GPU, sample tokensLesson 24 model forward
process_batch_resultAppend tokens, detect finished, free KV, send to detokenizeLesson 17 detokenize & streaming

Who decides, who computes: the single-threaded sole decision-maker

There are many roles in the engine, but each TP rank has exactly one scheduler, and it is single-threaded. That is not a limitation but a design: letting one role own the KV-cache ledger and decide every step's batch means no two parties fight over the same VRAM and no locks are needed. Telling "who decides" from "who computes" is the key to reading scheduler code:

Scheduler: decides, doesn't compute

One process, single-threaded, the sole decision-maker. It owns the KV-cache ledger, decides who is in this step's batch, who finishes and leaves, who fills in. It does only lightweight CPU accounting and judgment, touching not a single matmul — precisely because it is light, it can beat tens of thousands of times per second.

TpWorker / ModelRunner: computes, doesn't decide

It takes the batch the scheduler formed and dutifully runs forward on the GPU (Lesson 24), producing logits, then samples. It does not decide what the batch is nor touch scheduling policy — it just squeezes the compute. Decoupling decision from compute is what lets the two pipeline in parallel in the overlap scheduler.

Real code: the event_loop_normal heartbeat

Here is the actual loop skeleton. Notice how plain it is — just a while True with our five steps laid out verbatim: receive, enqueue, form batch, (if any) forward + finish, else idle self-check. Read these dozen lines and you've read the heartbeat of the entire engine.

python/sglang/srt/managers/scheduler.py ::Scheduler.event_loop_normalthe scheduler heartbeat: one revolution per step
def event_loop_normal(self):
    # A normal scheduler loop.
    while True:
        if self.gracefully_exit:
            break

        # (1) receive: drain the ZMQ inbox of new requests
        recv_reqs = self.request_receiver.recv_requests()
        # (2) enqueue: new reqs into waiting queue, handle control msgs
        self.process_input_requests(recv_reqs)

        # (3) form batch: decide what this step runs (prefill or decode)
        batch = self.get_next_batch_to_run()
        self.cur_batch = batch

        if batch:
            # (4) forward: run this batch on GPU, sample from logits
            result = self.run_batch(batch)
            # (5) finish: append tokens, evict finished, send to detok
            self.process_batch_result(batch, result)
        else:
            # nothing to do: self-check and re-init states
            self.on_idle()

        self.last_batch = batch

Line it up with the opening radar analogy: recv_requests is "take in arrivals," process_input_requests is "put them in the holding pattern," get_next_batch_to_run is "decide which planes this sweep," run_batch is "clear them to execute," and process_batch_result is "handle what just landed/departed." The radar sweeps again — back to the top of while True. event_loop_overlap is the pipelined version of the same logic: a result queue defers the previous step's process_batch_result to overlap with GPU compute, thereby hiding the CPU time (detailed in Lesson 21).

One final intuition: the loop's speed directly caps throughput. Each revolution advances exactly one decode step; every request emits exactly one token. If the Python accounting in steps 3 and 5 is too slow, the GPU idles waiting on the CPU to form the batch, and no card, however strong, stays fed. So SGLang polishes this loop as the hottest of hot paths — the entire reason Lesson 21's overlap scheduler exists, and the yardstick to carry through all of Part 5: every design ultimately serves one goal — keep this heart beating fast and full.

Draw the heartbeat as a wheel: four steps, spinning forever

Having read the prose, picture the closed loop as a wheel: four actions joined head-to-tail, always turning the same direction, and one full revolution is one step. Note the while True at the hub — it is the engine that keeps the wheel spinning forever.

Fig 1 · The scheduler event loop (a wheel) — four actions joined head-to-tail into a wheel: ① recv_requests receive → ② get_next_batch form batch → ③ run_batch forward on the GPU → ④ process_result emit result, then back to ①; the while True at the hub makes it turn once per step, spinning forever.

The batch is fluid: waiting queue → running batch → finished

The wheel drew the actions, not the requests. Here is the other view: waiting requests sit in a queue on the left, admitted ones enter the running batch in the center (forwarded once each step), and requests that hit a stop condition exit on the right and free their KV — while the running batch's members change every revolution, which is continuous batching in the flesh.

Fig 2 · queue → running batch → finished — waiting requests sit in the left queue; once admitted they enter the center running batch (forwarded once per step, membership ever-changing); requests that hit a stop condition exit on the right and free their KV slots; the batch composition therefore changes every revolution — continuous batching, physically.

run_batch: hand one batch to the worker for a single forward

Step 4, run_batch, is itself very thin — it hands the whole batch to the TpWorker for one forward, gets back logits and the sampled tokens, and bumps forward_ct by one. That counter is the running tally of how many heartbeats the engine has taken so far.

python/sglang/srt/managers/scheduler.py ::Scheduler.run_batchhand a batch to the model worker for one forward, get the next tokens
def run_batch(self, batch):
    self.forward_ct += 1
    # hand the batch to the model worker for ONE forward (prefill or decode);
    # get back the next-token logits and sampled ids
    logits_output, next_token_ids = self.tp_worker.forward_batch_generation(batch)
    return logits_output, next_token_ids

A concrete example (counting steps): say the server just started with forward_ct = 0. Step 1 forms a prefill batch of 3 requests, and after run_batch returns, forward_ct = 1; the next several steps run decode batches (each advancing every request by 1 token), and by step 100 forward_ct = 100 — the heart has beaten exactly 100 times by now.

Another example (batch size shifts): step 1's running batch = {req0, req1, req5}, batch size = 3; if req1 hits EOS this step and leaves while req6 is admitted from the waiting queue, then the next step's running batch becomes {req0, req5, req6} — still 3 members, but the membership changed. That is exactly Fig 2's point: the batch size may hold steady, yet its composition is reshuffled every revolution.

📌 Key points
  • The scheduler is the engine's heart: it runs in its own subprocess; its core is the infinite loop event_loop_normal, beating thousands of times per second, each beat a step.
  • The five stages of a step (memorize the order): recv_requests → process_input_requests → get_next_batch_to_run → run_batch → process_batch_result → back to the top.
  • The batch is re-formed every step: stage 3 reruns each revolution — finished reqs leave on the spot and free KV, waiting ones fill in — this is Lesson 5's continuous batching in the flesh.
  • Decide vs compute: the scheduler is single-threaded, the sole decision-maker, owns the KV ledger, does only light CPU accounting; only TpWorker/ModelRunner runs forward on the GPU (Lesson 24).
  • Loop speed caps throughput: CPU batching and GPU compute are serial in the normal version ⇒ Lesson 21's event_loop_overlap pipelines to hide CPU time behind GPU compute. Builds on Lessons 16/17; leads to Lessons 19/20/22/24.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. What five things does the scheduler's event_loop_normal do each step (one revolution), and why does the order matter?
  1. Order is irrelevant; the five steps can run in any shuffled, parallel order
  2. First run_batch then recv_requests, since you compute before receiving
  3. recv_requests (receive) → process_input_requests (into waiting queue) → get_next_batch_to_run (form this step's batch) → run_batch (GPU forward+sample) → process_batch_result (append tokens/evict finished/free KV/send to detok), then loop; the order matters because you must take in new reqs before forming a batch, form a batch before forwarding, and have results before finishing
  4. Only get_next_batch_to_run; everything else happens inside the GPU kernel
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. The loop is while True, and the five steps have strict data dependencies: no new reqs means nothing to enqueue, an empty waiting queue means no prefill batch, no batch means no forward, and no forward result means you can't append tokens, detect finish, or free KV. Shuffling breaks the dependency. Memorizing this order is the master index to Part 5.
2. Why does the scheduler re-call get_next_batch_to_run to re-form the batch every step, rather than forming it once and reusing it?
  1. Because this is where continuous batching (Lesson 5) physically happens: re-forming each step lets finished reqs leave on the spot and free their KV slots and waiting reqs fill in next step, keeping the batch full so the GPU never idles for any single request
  2. Because re-forming speeds up a single request's decoding
  3. Purely for logging convenience, with no performance meaning
  4. Because the GPU requires a brand-new batch object each step or it errors
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. Forming once and locking it degrades to static batching: the whole batch waits for the slowest req, finished slots idle, new reqs can't enter. Re-forming each step makes the batch a 'recomputed result' rather than a static object — finished filtered out, waiting admitted in, always full. That is continuous batching, the first engine of high throughput.
3. Within one step, which part is the scheduler's CPU decision and which is the actual GPU compute? How does this split motivate event_loop_overlap (Lesson 21)?
  1. The scheduler itself runs inside the GPU kernel, so overlap is moot
  2. get_next_batch_to_run / process_batch_result are pure-CPU lightweight accounting and decision, while run_batch (→ModelRunner.forward) is the GPU compute; the normal loop runs them serially so the GPU idles while the CPU forms the batch — hence Lesson 21 pipelines to hide the CPU part behind the previous step's GPU compute
  3. run_batch is the CPU decision, while forming the batch is the GPU compute
  4. All five steps run on the GPU; no CPU involvement
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. The scheduler 'decides, never computes': batching and finishing are light CPU accounting, while only run_batch hands the batch to TpWorker→ModelRunner for GPU forward (Lesson 24). The normal loop is strictly serial — CPU busy means GPU idle and vice versa. Since loop speed caps throughput, Lesson 21's overlap version defers the previous step's finishing via a result queue to overlap this step's GPU compute, hiding the CPU time.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • Using the 'air-traffic controller's radar sweep' analogy, describe one full step of the scheduler's event_loop_normal. Map each of the five stages: recv_requests (receive TokenizedGenerateReqInput, Lesson 16), process_input_requests (enqueue into waiting, handle abort/flush), get_next_batch_to_run (prefill first else decode, Lessons 19/20), run_batch (→ModelRunner.forward, Lesson 24), and process_batch_result (append tokens, detect finished, free KV, send to DetokenizerManager, Lesson 17), and explain why the 'loop-back' is the soul of the heartbeat.
  • Argue the claim 'loop speed directly caps throughput,' and explain why the scheduler is single-threaded, the sole decision-maker per TP rank, owning the KV-cache ledger. Building on that, explain how event_loop_overlap (Lesson 21) optimizes via pipelining, and why a long prompt needs chunked prefill (Lesson 22) to avoid clogging this loop.

Req & ScheduleBatch

Last lesson we saw the scheduler's heartbeat—that while True event loop (Lesson 18). But what does each beat actually operate on? The answer is two data structures that run through all of Part 5: Req and ScheduleBatch. Req is one request's own state machine—it stays alive from birth to death; ScheduleBatch is the cluster of Reqs forwarded together in ONE step, and it is ephemeral—rebuilt every beat. Grasp this "persistent vs temporary" split and you hold the two tools in the scheduler's hands.

🔌 Analogy
Picture the engine as an airline. Each passenger (Req) carries a persistent booking and state: where they board, where they go, what they checked, whether they've checked in, boarded, arrived. That record persists from booking to landing, surviving many flight legs. But each departing flight manifest (ScheduleBatch) is assembled per departure: it is either a boarding group of new passengers (extend/prefill batch—seating new arrivals by how much luggage they carry) or a cruising cabin of existing passengers (decode batch—everyone advancing one step together). Passengers are persistent, manifests are assembled per flight and discarded on landing; whoever arrives deplanes (filter_batch removes finished requests from the manifest). The SAME passenger appears on many successive cruising manifests—exactly why Req and ScheduleBatch must be split.
🌍 Big picture
In one line: Req records "who a request is and how far it got"; ScheduleBatch records "which Reqs to compute together this step, and how." Req holds input tokens, output tokens generated so far, sampling params, the KV-cache indices it owns (Lessons 4/30), prefix-match info (RadixAttention, Lesson 7), and finished/abort status; it moves through waiting → running → finished. ScheduleBatch carries a mode: either EXTEND (prefill)—admit new requests and consume their whole prompt at once—or DECODE—let running requests each emit one new token. The batch also handles KV-pool alloc (on admit) and free (on finish), and filter_batch() drops finished requests mid-flight (the "slot freeing" of continuous batching, Lesson 5).

1. Req: one request's state machine, alive from birth to death

Req is SGLang's most intimate object—it is "a request's entire net worth." The class's docstring is one line: "The input and output status of a request." It holds at least: original input tokens (origin_input_ids), incrementally appended output tokens (output_ids), sampling params (sampling_params), the position it occupies in the KV cache (req_pool_idx, kv_committed_len/kv_allocated_len, ...), and, on a prefix-tree hit, its prefix_indices (RadixAttention, Lesson 7). Crucially it carries its own lifecycle: before admission it queues in waiting; once chosen for prefill it enters running; after emitting its last token (EOS hit, max_new_tokens reached, or abort) it becomes finished, and its KV slots are freed. Here is its whole life.

1

waiting

The request is tokenized and sent into the scheduler over IPC (Lesson 16), landing first in the waiting queue. It owns no KV yet—just queuing, waiting for the schedule policy (Lesson 20) to pick it.

2

running · first beat: extend (prefill)

Once selected it enters running and, in an EXTEND batch, consumes its whole prompt at once: prepare_for_extend() allocates its KV slots and lays out variable-length token tensors. Prefix-hit parts (Lesson 7) are reused, not recomputed.

3

running · then many beats: decode

The same Req then appears in a long run of DECODE batches; each beat prepare_for_decode() requests one new KV slot and computes just one new token, appending one to output_ids. It dwells here longest.

4

finished / freed

On EOS, max_new_tokens, or abort it turns finished; filter_batch() drops it from the current batch on the spot, KV slots return to the pool (Lesson 30), and results flow to the detokenizer (Lesson 17).

Fig 19A · A Req's lifecycle — waiting in queue → running, first prefill eats the whole prompt, then decode adds +1 each beat → finished (hits stop, or reaches length); throughout, the Req keeps carrying origin_input_ids and a growing output_ids.
python/sglang/srt/managers/schedule_batch.py ::Reqone request's full running state inside the scheduler
class Req:
    # one request's full running state inside the scheduler
    def __init__(self, rid, origin_input_ids, sampling_params, ...):
        self.rid = rid
        self.origin_input_ids = origin_input_ids  # the prompt token ids
        self.output_ids = []                       # tokens generated so far
        self.sampling_params = sampling_params
        self.finished_reason = None                # set when the request stops
        # ... prefix-cache node, KV indices, etc.

As a concrete example: when generation hits EOS, finished_reason becomes FINISH_MATCHED_TOKEN (i.e. stop); when it reaches max_new_tokens=256, it becomes FINISH_LENGTH (i.e. length). Likewise, if one beat's ScheduleBatch has len(reqs)=8, then those 8 requests are packed into one batch and forwarded together.

2. ScheduleBatch: the cluster forwarded together this step, carrying a mode

If Req is "a person," ScheduleBatch is "this flight's manifest"—docstring: "Store all information of a batch on the scheduler." Its first field is literally reqs: List[Req], the requests to compute this step; almost all other fields are tensors laid out temporarily for the forward pass (input_ids, seq_lens, out_cache_loc, ...). Its most important property is forward_mode, with two main forms that decide "how" this beat computes:

EXTEND / prefill batch

Built by prepare_for_extend(), forward_mode = EXTEND. It admits new requests and forwards each request's whole prompt at once. Because prompts differ in length, it lays out variable-length (ragged) token tensors and allocates KV slots for each new request. In source, input_ids takes get_fill_ids()[len(prefix_indices):]— only the part the prefix missed (Lesson 7). When a long prompt is too big, it is sliced into chunked prefill (Lesson 22).

DECODE batch

Built by prepare_for_decode(), forward_mode = DECODE. It holds already-running requests, each emitting just one new token, so tensors are the regular 1 token per request, needing only one more KV slot per request for the new token. It recurs constantly—a request spends the vast majority of its beats in decode batches. This is throughput's main stage: the fuller the batch, the better the GPU pays off (continuous batching, Lesson 5).

Fig 19B · ScheduleBatch packs several Reqs — a few different-length Reqs (A/B/C) are placed into one ScheduleBatch and computed by the model in a single forward; the batch's tensors input_ids, positions, out_cache_loc are shared by everyone this beat and discarded after use.

3. Key fields at a glance: what each holds, what role it plays

Lay the two structures' core fields side by side and the "persistent vs temporary" split is obvious—Req's fields travel with the request, ScheduleBatch's fields travel with this step.

StructureFieldRole
Reqorigin_input_ids / output_idsInput tokens and incrementally appended output tokens—the request's "content," persistent
Reqsampling_paramsTemperature, top-p, max_new_tokens, ...—decides how to pick the next token
Reqreq_pool_idx / kv_*_lenIts position and length in the KV cache (Lessons 4/30), growing as it generates
Reqprefix_indicesPrefix-tree reuse (RadixAttention, Lesson 7), skipped during extend
Reqfinished() statusEOS / max_new_tokens / abort—triggers filter_batch eviction and KV free
ScheduleBatchreqs: List[Req]The requests forwarded together this step—the batch's "skeleton," rebuilt each step
ScheduleBatchforward_modeEXTEND or DECODE—decides "how" this beat computes
ScheduleBatchinput_ids / seq_lens / out_cache_locTensors laid out for the forward pass and KV output locations, handed to ModelRunner (Lesson 24)

4. What a decode batch looks like: a few requests, each +1 this beat

Zoom into one decode beat: the batch has several Reqs, their sequences grown to different lengths, and each adds exactly one new token this step. Each row below is one request; the highlighted cell is the single token generated this beat and just written to KV.

A DECODE batch (this beat: each request +1 new token)
Req AThe weather is nice+1
Req Bdef add(a+1
Req CThe sky is very blue+1

Note the three requests have different generated lengths yet sit in one decode batch—because decode tensors are the regular "1 token per request," with length differences recorded by each seq_lens. Next beat, if Req B emits EOS, filter_batch() drops it on the spot and frees its KV slots, leaving A and C running; the freed capacity can then admit new requests from the waiting queue (Lessons 5 and 20).

python/sglang/srt/managers/schedule_batch.py ::ScheduleBatchskeletons of the two data structures
class Req(ReqDllmMixin):
    # The input and output status of a request.
    def __init__(self, rid, origin_input_ids, sampling_params, ...):
        self.rid = rid                      # request id
        self.origin_input_ids = origin_input_ids   # input tokens
        self.output_ids = array("q")         # incrementally appended output
        self.sampling_params = sampling_params     # sampling params
        self.req_pool_idx = None            # KV slot it owns (Lessons 4/30)

class ScheduleBatch(ScheduleBatchDisaggregationDecodeMixin):
    # Store all information of a batch on the scheduler.
    reqs: List[Req]                         # requests forwarded together this step

    def prepare_for_extend(self):           # prefill: admit new reqs, eat whole prompt
        self.forward_mode = ForwardMode.EXTEND
        # only the part the prefix missed (RadixAttention, Lesson 7)
        input_ids = [r.get_fill_ids()[len(r.prefix_indices):] for r in self.reqs]

    def prepare_for_decode(self):           # decode: running reqs, +1 token each
        self.forward_mode = ForwardMode.DECODE
        # one more KV slot per req; tensors regular, 1 token per req

    def filter_batch(self, ...):            # drop finished reqs mid-flight (Lesson 5)
        keep_indices = [i for i in range(len(self.reqs))
                        if not self.reqs[i].finished()]
        self.reqs = [self.reqs[i] for i in keep_indices]
📌 Key points
  • Req = one request's state machine, persistent: holds input/output tokens, sampling params, owned KV indices (Lessons 4/30), prefix-match info (Lesson 7), and finished status; moves through waiting → running → finished, alive from birth to death.
  • ScheduleBatch = the cluster of Reqs forwarded together this step, temporary: its first field is reqs: List[Req], the rest mostly tensors laid out for the forward pass; it is rebuilt every step (Lesson 18) and discarded after use.
  • The batch has two modes: EXTEND/prefill (prepare_for_extend(), admit new reqs, eat whole prompt, lay out ragged tensors) vs DECODE (prepare_for_decode(), running reqs +1 token each, regular tensors).
  • The batch manages KV alloc and free: alloc on admit, free on finish; filter_batch() drops finished reqs mid-flight, freeing slots for waiters—this is continuous batching (Lesson 5) in the flesh.
  • Why split them: the same Req appears in many successive decode batches—Req persists, the batch is ephemeral. Carry this pair into the schedule policy (Lesson 20), chunked prefill (Lesson 22), and model forward (Lesson 24), and all of Part 5 lines up.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. What is the most essential lifecycle difference between Req and ScheduleBatch?
  1. Both are temporary, rebuilt per token, mirroring each other
  2. Req is persistent—one request stays alive from waiting to finished; ScheduleBatch is ephemeral—rebuilt every step and discarded, so the same Req appears in many successive decode batches
  3. Both are persistent, with exactly one instance each per session
  4. Req is temporary and ScheduleBatch persistent, the batch lasting until engine shutdown
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. This is exactly why they are split: the batch is a recomputed result of each loop step (Lesson 18), ephemeral; while a request takes tens to hundreds of decode beats to finish, needing a persistent object to carry its output tokens, KV indices, and status throughout. Req persistent, batch ephemeral—the spine of Part 5.
2. What is the essential difference between a ScheduleBatch's EXTEND (prefill) batch and a DECODE batch?
  1. DECODE admits new requests and processes whole prompts, EXTEND emits just one token
  2. They are identical, differing only in name
  3. EXTEND, built by prepare_for_extend(), admits new requests and eats each whole prompt at once (ragged tensors, allocate KV for new reqs); DECODE, built by prepare_for_decode(), gives each running request +1 token (regular 1-token-per-req, one more KV slot each)
  4. EXTEND runs on GPU, DECODE on CPU, unrelated to each other
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. forward_mode decides 'how' this beat computes. EXTEND handles the prompt: varying lengths ⇒ ragged tensors, allocate KV for new reqs, and only the prefix-missed part (Lesson 7). DECODE handles generation: each emits one token ⇒ regular tensors, one more KV slot each. A request extends once in its life (or split into chunks, Lesson 22) but decodes many times.
3. What does filter_batch() do in scheduling, and why is it crucial for throughput?
  1. It sorts the whole batch so the longest request comes first
  2. It drops finished requests from the batch on the spot mid-flight and frees their KV slots, so the freed capacity lets new requests from the waiting queue fill in—the 'slot-freeing' of continuous batching (Lesson 5)
  3. It copies the batch as a backup and never removes any request
  4. It is called once at engine shutdown to clear all requests
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. In source, filter_batch keeps only requests that are not finished() via keep_indices, rebuilding reqs and the matching tensors. Without dropping finished ones mid-flight, the batch degrades to static batching—waiting for the slowest, finished slots idle. Evict on the spot + free KV + admit waiters keeps the batch full, the root of high throughput.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • Using the 'airline: passenger (Req) vs flight manifest (ScheduleBatch)' analogy, explain the division of labor between the two data structures. Cover: which fields Req carries (input/output tokens, sampling params, KV indices, prefix_indices, finished status) and its waiting→running→finished lifecycle; and why ScheduleBatch is rebuilt each step, plus how its two forward_modes (EXTEND/DECODE) lay out tensors and allocate KV.
  • Thinking forward from this lesson: get_next_batch_to_run (Lessons 18/20) must choose each step between 'form an EXTEND batch to admit new requests' and 'form a DECODE batch to advance running ones.' Explain how this choice interacts with KV-pool capacity, waiting-queue length, and the empty slots freed by filter_batch, and connect it to why chunked prefill (Lesson 22) prevents one very long prompt from clogging this loop.

The schedule policy

Last lesson we nailed two data structures: the persistent Req and the ephemeral ScheduleBatch. This lesson answers the two most critical questions of every step: with so many requests waiting, who runs next, and how many fit? The answers live in two roles—SchedulePolicy does the ordering (who first), PrefillAdder does the throttling (how many). The former lets requests whose prefix is already in cache jump the line, harvesting nearly-free throughput; the latter holds two yardsticks—a token budget and a memory budget—and stuffs requests into this step's prefill batch one by one until nothing more fits. The scheduler and the cache (Lesson 7) are co-designed this way—the secret behind SGLang being fast and cheap under real traffic.

🔌 Real-world analogy
Think of the schedule policy as a smart venue host / bouncer at a hot restaurant. A long line waits outside, and he does not naively admit people purely by arrival. First he reorders the line: which parties are "already half-prepped" (ingredients ready = cache hit, i.e. LPM, longest-prefix-match) get bumped to the front— serving them costs the kitchen almost nothing; if everyone's similar, he admits by arrival (FCFS), fair and simple; and VIP reservations go early by explicit priority. Once ordered, he admits people counting two capacities: are there enough seats (the memory KV budget), and how many dishes can the kitchen cook this round (the token budget)— whichever fills first stops the round. Next beat, repeat. Order + throttle is the whole of the schedule policy.
🌍 The big picture
In one line: policy decides order, budget decides count. get_next_batch_to_run (Lesson 18) each beat first calls SchedulePolicy.calc_priority to reorder the waiting queue in place, then hands off to PrefillAdder to add_one_req one by one under a double budget, admitting where it fits (chunking if needed) and stopping when full. The core ordering mode is cache-aware LPM: prefer requests whose prompt prefix already sits in the RadixAttention cache— admitting them is nearly "free" because that attention was already computed (Lesson 7). This step directly raises the cache hit rate, and hit rate is where SGLang's real speed comes from. Meanwhile the scheduler each beat balances "admit new prefills" vs "just decode": the former grows throughput but raises latency for running decodes, the latter keeps latency low (Lesson 8). The policy is the weight on this scale, beat after beat.

Who runs first: what each of the three policies optimizes

SchedulePolicy abstracts "order" into one call to calc_priority: it reorders the waiting queue in place. The mode is chosen by workload—when many requests share prefixes, cache-aware pays off most; for fairness and simplicity, FCFS; to honor business priority, priority. Note one engineering detail: when the queue is very long (>128), the expensive prefix matching temporarily falls back to FCFS, so sorting itself never becomes the bottleneck.

PolicyHow it ordersWhat it optimizesCost / fit
LPM (cache-aware)Longest-prefix-match first: cached-prefix reqs jump aheadCache hit rate → near-free throughputMust compute prefixes; falls back to FCFS on long queues
FCFSFirst-come-first-served, by arrivalFair, simple, predictableIgnores cache; misses hot-prefix dividends
prioritySort by each request's explicit priorityHonors business urgency (VIP first)Needs upper-layer values; can starve low priority

How many fit: PrefillAdder builds a batch under a double budget

Once ordered, PrefillAdder holds two yardsticks and stuffs requests into this beat's prefill batch: a token budget (rem_total_tokens / rem_input_tokens, keep this prefill step from getting too big—tied to chunked prefill, Lesson 22), and a memory budget (enough free KV-pool slots, Lessons 4/30—never admit a request you can't fit). add_one_req tries each in turn: add it whole if it fits, otherwise chunk it; once either budget is exhausted it returns NO_TOKEN / OTHER and stops.

1

Take the head request

Pull the next req off the sorted waiting queue and compute its token need: cand_extend_input_len + max_new + page_size.

2

Check the token budget

If total_tokens ≥ rem_total_tokens, return NO_TOKEN; this yardstick caps the total prefill per beat.

3

Check memory / slots

Insufficient KV-pool room (incl. SWA constraints) also rejects—never over-admit a request that can't fit.

4

Add whole or chunk

If the budget allows, add the whole req to can_run_list; if only half fits, chunk it and continue next beat (Lesson 22).

5

Update budget, back to 1

_update_prefill_budget deducts both budgets; loop until full or the queue empties—this beat's batch is then fixed.

Cache-aware vs fair: LPM against FCFS

Why does cache-aware ordering matter so much? Because it directly raises the RadixAttention hit rate—by clustering "same opening" requests to run first, the shared prefix is computed once. That is the meaning of co-designing the scheduler with the cache: the goal of ordering is not abstract fairness but feeding the cache—the money-saving machine—as full as possible. But cache-awareness has a cost: computing prefixes, and possibly making later arrivals wait a bit longer. So FCFS is kept as the counterpoint—simple, fair, predictable.

LPM (cache-aware)

Bumps prefix-hit requests to the front. Pros: high hit rate, near-free admission, huge real-traffic speedups (shared system prompts / few-shot / chat history). Cost: must compute prefixes; falls back to FCFS on long queues. Optimizes hit rate.

FCFS (first-come-first-served)

Strictly by arrival, first in first run. Pros: fair, simple, predictable latency, no infinite wait for late arrivals. Cost: ignores the cache, leaking the hot-prefix dividend. Optimizes fairness.

The line-jump in action: a reordered waiting queue

This diagram grounds the abstract "ordering": the original queue by arrival is A, B, C, D; among them B and D have cache-hitting openings (half-set tables). LPM bumps them to the front—this beat runs B and D first, harvesting two nearly-free units of throughput, and A, C slide back. The highlighted cells are the prefix-hit requests that jump ahead.

Waiting queue: LPM bumps cache-hitting requests (highlighted) to the front
RawA arrivesB arrivesC arrivesD arrivesFCFS order
Hit?A missB prefix hitC missD prefix hitprobe cache
ReorderedB firstD firstA afterC afterLPM order

Draw the ordering: LPM reorder and three policies compared

The text kept saying "cache-aware ordering"; the two figures below make it concrete: Fig 1 shows how LPM bumps requests that share a prefix to the front, and Fig 2 lines up FCFS, LPM, and priority side by side so you can see at a glance what each optimizes, its cost, and where it fits.

Fig 1 · LPM reorders the queue by prefix hit — the original queue by arrival is A, B, C, D; B and D's prefix P already sits in the radix cache, so LPM follows the cache hit hint and bumps them to the front to run together, the shared prefix is computed once, A and C slide back, harvesting two nearly-free units of throughput.

A concrete number example: of 1000 live requests, 600 begin with the same 2000-token system prompt. Admitted out of order under FCFS, those 600 are separated by misses and hit the cache piecemeal—the hot prefix may even be evicted by other requests and recomputed; under LPM they are clustered together and run first, so that 2000-token attention is computed once and the other 599 reuse it directly— this one effect alone cuts a large share of this batch's prefill compute, turning the hit rate from "luck of the draw" into "steadily maxed out".

Fig 2 · policies compared — FCFS seeks fairness, simple and predictable but misses the cache dividend; LPM is cache-aware and maximizes prefix reuse to raise the hit rate (at the cost of computing prefixes and falling back to FCFS on long queues); priority honors business urgency but can starve low-priority requests. Pick the yardstick by workload.

A smaller example to feel why "clustering" matters: the queue [chatA, docX, chatB, docY, chatC] is interleaved, and the three "chat" requests share one conversation system prompt. LPM reorders it to [chatA, chatB, chatC, docX, docY]— the three chats run back-to-back so that system-prompt prefix stays hot in the cache and is never evicted by the doc requests, keeping the hit alive throughout; without reordering, every doc request run in between can evict the chat prefix, forcing the next chat to recompute it and quietly dragging the hit rate down. That is the plainest power of "clustering".

python/sglang/srt/managers/schedule_policy.py ::CacheAwarePolicycache-aware scheduling policies: order the queue using the radix cache
class CacheAwarePolicy(Enum):
    # cache-aware policies: use the radix cache to decide who runs next
    LPM = "lpm"            # longest-prefix-match: prefer requests sharing a cached prefix
    DFS_WEIGHT = "dfs-weight"
    # (vs CacheAgnosticPolicy: FCFS, etc. when the cache can't help)

Read the source: calc_priority and the budget check

Both the policy and the budget live in schedule_policy.py. calc_priority first determines the active policy, then sorts by branch; PrefillAdder.add_one_req decides admit-or-reject through a chain of budget checks. Below are the most telling lines.

schedule_policy.py ::SchedulePolicy.calc_priorityorder + throttle
def calc_priority(self, waiting_queue, running_batch=None):
    policy = self._determine_active_policy(waiting_queue)   # long queue(>128) falls back to FCFS

    if self.policy == CacheAgnosticPolicy.FCFS:          # fair: by arrival order
        if self.enable_priority_scheduling:
            SchedulePolicy._sort_by_priority_and_fcfs(waiting_queue, self.priority_sign)
        return

    if isinstance(policy, CacheAwarePolicy):           # cache-aware: raise hit rate
        temporary_deprioritized = self._compute_prefix_matches(waiting_queue, policy)
        if policy == CacheAwarePolicy.LPM:               # longest-prefix-match first
            SchedulePolicy._sort_by_longest_prefix(waiting_queue, temporary_deprioritized)

# —— PrefillAdder.add_one_req: the double-budget check ——
total_tokens = cand_extend_input_len + max_new + self.page_size
if total_tokens >= self.rem_total_tokens:            # token/memory budget short -> reject
    return AddReqResult.NO_TOKEN                       # never admit one that won't fit (L4/30)
📌 Key points
  • Policy ordering and budget throttling are two things: SchedulePolicy.calc_priority decides who runs first (reorders the waiting queue in place), PrefillAdder decides how many fit (add_one_req under a double budget).
  • Three modes, each with a strength: LPM (cache-aware) optimizes hit rate by bumping prefix-cached reqs ahead (Lesson 7); FCFS optimizes fairness, simple and predictable; priority honors business priority. Pick LPM when the workload shares prefixes.
  • PrefillAdder's two yardsticks: a token budget (keep a beat's prefill from getting too big, tied to chunked prefill in Lesson 22) + a memory budget (free KV-pool slots, Lessons 4/30); stop when either is exhausted, chunk if needed.
  • The prefill-vs-decode admission tension: each beat balances "admit new prefills to grow throughput but raise latency" vs "just decode to keep latency low" (Lessons 18/8); the policy is the weight on this scale.
  • Scheduler and cache are co-designed: cache-aware ordering directly raises the RadixAttention hit rate, and that hit rate is the root of SGLang's real-traffic speedup—ordering's goal is not abstract fairness but feeding the cache full.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. What does cache-aware LPM (longest-prefix-match) ordering optimize, versus FCFS?
  1. LPM optimizes cache hit rate—bumping requests whose prompt prefix is already in the RadixAttention cache to the front so admitting them is nearly free; FCFS optimizes fairness—strictly by arrival, simple and predictable
  2. Both only optimize single-request latency, with no effect on throughput
  3. LPM optimizes fairness and FCFS optimizes hit rate, exactly reversed
  4. Both optimize memory usage, unrelated to the cache
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. LPM lets cached-opening requests jump ahead so a shared prefix is computed once, directly raising the RadixAttention hit rate—the source of real-traffic speedups (Lesson 7); FCFS ignores the cache and goes by arrival, buying fairness, simplicity and predictable latency. On long queues LPM even falls back to FCFS so sorting never becomes the bottleneck.
2. When PrefillAdder decides 'how many requests fit this beat', which two budgets does it use?
  1. A token budget (keep this prefill step from getting too big, tied to chunked prefill) + a memory budget (enough free KV-pool slots, never admit a req that won't fit); stop when either runs out
  2. A disk-space budget + a process-count budget
  3. A CPU-core budget + a network-bandwidth budget
