  4. Only a token budget; memory never enters the decision
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. add_one_req computes total_tokens = cand_extend_input_len + max_new + page_size; if ≥ rem_total_tokens it returns NO_TOKEN, and it also checks free KV-pool slots (Lessons 4/30). The token budget caps the per-beat prefill size (chunking, Lesson 22), the memory budget guarantees it fits—whichever yardstick fills first stops admission.
3. What is the 'prefill-vs-decode admission tension' the scheduler faces each beat?
  1. They are fully independent and never affect each other
  2. Prefill and decode must run on different GPUs; the scheduler just picks the card
  3. The tension refers to bandwidth contention between CPU and disk
  4. Admitting more new prefills grows throughput but raises latency for running decodes; just advancing old decodes keeps latency low but adds no new throughput—the policy balances the two each beat
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. Prefill eats whole prompts and burns compute, pulling new requests into the batch to grow throughput, but it contends with running decodes and raises their latency; decode-only keeps latency steady but adds no throughput. The scheduler weights this scale with policy each beat (Lessons 18/8)—the core trade-off of batch formation.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • Using the 'smart host / bouncer' analogy, explain the two actions of the schedule policy: ordering (how SchedulePolicy.calc_priority reorders the waiting queue in place by LPM / FCFS / priority) and throttling (how PrefillAdder calls add_one_req one by one under the token + memory double budget, chunking when needed). Then explain why cache-aware ordering directly raises the RadixAttention hit rate, illustrating the scheduler-cache co-design.
  • Thinking forward: if your production traffic has thousands of requests sharing one very long system prompt, would you pick LPM or FCFS, and why? Then, using PrefillAdder's double budget, explain how a very long prompt is held back by the token budget and chunked prefill (Lesson 22)—so it can't monopolize a beat and clog all decodes—connecting it to KV-pool capacity (Lessons 4/30) and the throughput/latency trade-off (Lesson 8).

Zero-overhead overlap scheduler

This is Part 5's signature lesson—the optimization name-dropped back in Lessons 1 and 3: the zero-overhead overlap scheduler. Recall Lesson 18: every scheduler step mixes two kinds of work—CPU scheduling (build the batch, sample, bookkeep, prepare the next step's inputs) and the GPU forward. In the naive event_loop_normal, these are strictly serial: GPU finishes → CPU then forms the next batch → GPU runs again. The catch: while the CPU forms the batch, the GPU sits idle. For decode—where each step is very fast—the fixed CPU overhead becomes a sizable fraction of a step, so a chunk of expensive GPU time is wasted. This lesson shows how SGLang pipelines the two to erase that idle gap entirely: keep the GPU never idle.

🔌 Real-world analogy
Think of one step as a relay race / two-stage assembly line. The naive loop is a single runner: after sprinting this leg he must stop, walk back to the line, tie his shoes, catch his breath—and only then sprint the next leg, leaving the track (GPU) empty in between. The overlap scheduler uses two runners: while the current runner (GPU) sprints all-out, the next runner (CPU) is already at the line, laced up and set, so the baton passes with zero pause. Or a kitchen: while the current dish sizzles on the stove (GPU), the prep cook (CPU) is already chopping and plating the next order— the stove never burns empty. The only cost: you are forever one beat behind, because the work you finish is always the previous leg's result.
🌍 The big picture
In one line: hide this step's CPU scheduling inside the previous step's GPU compute. Instead of "compute → finish → form next" in series, event_loop_overlap launches step N's GPU forward, does not wait for it, and immediately has the CPU form step N+1's batch and finish the previous (N−1) step's result— the previous result lives in a result_queue (a future) and is processed one beat late. Each step's CPU overhead now falls neatly within the "shadow" of that step's GPU compute and is fully hidden, presenting as near-zero scheduling overhead with the GPU saturated. The cost is one extra beat of latency (you're always one step behind) plus finer state management—the next batch is built before the current result is known, so sampled tokens and KV bookkeeping have cross-beat dependencies, which SGLang handles for you via the FutureMap in overlap_utils.py (the result_queue deque lives in scheduler.py itself). This is v0.4's "zero-overhead batch scheduler"; it pairs perfectly with continuous batching (Lesson 5): the batch is always full, and the scheduling that keeps it full costs nothing.

The problem: in the serial loop, the GPU idles while the CPU forms the batch

Do the accounting first. The only thing that truly burns GPU in a step is run_batch (→ ModelRunner.forward, Lesson 24); the rest—get_next_batch_to_run (form the batch), sample, process_batch_result (append tokens, detect finished, free KV, bookkeep, prep next inputs)— is all lightweight CPU work. The naive event_loop_normal lines them up in a straight line: CPU forms batch → GPU forward → CPU finishes → CPU forms next → GPU forward… The trouble is at the seams: whenever the CPU is forming/sampling/bookkeeping, the GPU has nothing to do and just idles; conversely, the CPU idles during the forward. Two expensive resources take turns being busy and idle—neither is fed. For prefill (GPU-heavy) the CPU share is small; but once you hit decode— one token per request per step, an extremely fast forward—that fixed scheduling overhead becomes a conspicuous slice of the step. The faster the loop spins and the less the GPU rests, the more this fixed overhead stings: it caps whole-engine throughput.

Naive serial: event_loop_normal (Lesson 18)

Within a step, CPU forms batch → GPU forward → CPU finishes queue up with no overlap. The GPU idles for the whole stretch while the CPU forms/samples/bookkeeps. Decode steps make the GPU very fast, so CPU overhead dominates and compute is wasted; loop speed caps throughput.

Overlap pipeline: event_loop_overlap (this lesson)

Launch this step's GPU forward (don't wait), and the CPU immediately forms the next batch and finishes the previous step. CPU scheduling is hidden in the GPU compute's shadow, so the GPU has no gaps—scheduling overhead near zero. Cost: one beat behind.

The fix: tuck the next step's scheduling into this step's GPU shadow

The overlap loop has just three core moves, but the order is everything. First, launch step N's forward: call run_batch to hand the batch to the GPU, but return immediately—don't block on the result—and append this "launched, pending" batch with its result handle onto result_queue. Second, while the GPU is busy on N, the CPU goes off to form step N+1's batch (next step's get_next_batch_to_run is effectively prepped here). Third, finish the previous (N−1) step: popleft from result_queue that prior batch and its now-ready result and run process_batch_result— append tokens, detect finished, free KV. Note the stagger: when we process N−1's result, the GPU is busy computing N. So every slice of CPU work lands inside the GPU compute's shadow. That's where "one beat behind" comes from, and where "zero-overhead" comes from—you always process the previous step's result, yet no step's CPU time ever makes the GPU wait.

1

Launch step N's forward, don't wait

batch = get_next_batch_to_run() forms batch N; run_batch(batch) fires it onto the GPU and moves on, pushing (batch.copy(), result) onto result_queue. GPU starts; CPU doesn't block.

2

While GPU is busy, CPU preps N+1

With the GPU still on N, the CPU is already receiving requests, forming the next batch, prepping samples. This CPU time doesn't occupy the GPU—it hides in N's compute shadow.

3

Finish the previous step (N−1)

popleft batch N−1 from result_queue—it's now done. process_batch_result appends tokens, detects finished, frees KV, sends to detok (Lesson 17). You always process the previous step.

4

Update last_batch, back to 1

last_batch = batch; loop back to launch N+1. Every beat is a staggered "launch this / finish previous" pipeline; the GPU baton passes with no pause.

The GPU never idles: draw every beat on a timeline

This figure spreads "one beat behind" out in the open. Slice time into four beats (T1…T4); the top row is the GPU, the bottom row the CPU. In the naive serial case, the two rows are out of phase—GPU lit while CPU dark, CPU lit while GPU dark, always one side idling. In the overlap case, the GPU row is lit end to end with no empty cell (highlighted cell = "GPU computing"), while the CPU row works in the same beat as the GPU: at T2, the GPU computes batch 2 while the CPU simultaneously forms batch 3 and finishes batch 1's result. You can see two things at a glance: one, the GPU never idles—the root of saturated throughput; two, results always land one beat late—batch 1's result isn't finished until T2, which is exactly the extra beat of latency (Lesson 8's throughput/latency trade-off in the flesh).

Naive serial vs overlap pipeline: highlighted cell = GPU computing (watch whether the GPU row has gaps)
serial·GPUT1 run b1T2 idleT3 run b2T4 idlegaps → wasted compute
serial·CPUT1 idleT2 form b2T3 idleT4 form b3rows out of phase
overlap·GPUT1 run b1T2 run b2T3 run b3T4 run b4no gaps → saturated
overlap·CPUT1 form b2T2 form b3·finish b1T3 form b4·finish b2T4 form b5·finish b3hidden in GPU shadow
Fig 1 · Serial vs overlap (timeline) — in serial the GPU and CPU are out of phase and the GPU idles while the CPU forms/processes; in overlap the GPU row is full end to end with no gap, and the CPU's batch-forming and finishing hide inside the GPU compute's shadow.
Fig 2 · Overlap pipeline (one step out of phase) — within one time slot the GPU computes batch N while the CPU simultaneously builds batch N+1 and finishes batch N−1; the three lanes are staggered by exactly one step (one step out of phase), so no side ever waits.

A concrete number: in one decode beat the GPU forward takes about 8 ms and CPU scheduling about 4 ms. Serial makes the beat 12 ms with the GPU only 8/12 ≈ 67% utilized; overlap tucks those 4 ms of CPU work into the GPU's 8 ms shadow, shrinking the beat back to 8 ms and pushing GPU utilization to ≈ 100%—the same card yields roughly 50% more throughput, at the cost of one one-time extra beat (one step out of phase).

Account honestly: zero-overhead is not zero-cost

Overlap is no free lunch; it trades GPU idling for one beat of latency plus complexity. The upside column is hard: near-zero scheduling overhead, saturated GPU utilization, and clearly higher throughput in decode-heavy regimes. The cost column matters too: +1 beat of latency—you're forever one step behind, nudging single-request token timings (Lesson 8); and harder state management—the next batch is built before the current result exists, so sampled tokens and KV bookkeeping carry cross-beat dependencies, which SGLang threads through the FutureMap in overlap_utils.py to keep correct. Some cases (where you must have the previous result before forming the next, the source's is_disable_overlap_for_batch) temporarily fall back to no-overlap and finish the prior batch first. Understand this ledger and you see why it's "zero-overhead": not cost-free, but with the cost moved somewhere that doesn't dent throughput.

DimensionNaive event_loop_normalOverlap event_loop_overlap
CPU scheduling overheadOn the critical path; GPU idles≈ 0 (hidden in GPU shadow)
GPU utilizationVisible gaps on decode stepsSaturated, no gaps
ThroughputCapped by loop speedMarkedly higher (v0.4 headline)
LatencyFinished in real time+1 beat (always one behind)
Implementation complexitySimple, straight-lineNeeds result_queue + cross-beat deps

Read the source: event_loop_overlap's staggered pipeline

The real thing lives in scheduler.py's event_loop_overlap. Grab three points: one, result_queue, a deque holding "launched but not yet finished" batches; two, right after run_batch we append immediately, never blocking, deferring the finish to the next beat; three, pop_and_process takes the previous beat's batch off the front and runs process_batch_result—and while that line executes, the GPU is busy on this beat. That's how the stagger is built.

scheduler.py ::Scheduler.event_loop_overlapstaggered / one beat behind
def event_loop_overlap(self):
    # A loop that overlaps CPU scheduling with GPU compute
    self.result_queue = deque()                  # holds "launched, pending-finish" batches

    def pop_and_process():
        # Finish the previous step (N-1) result -- deferred one beat
        tmp_batch, tmp_result = self.result_queue.popleft()
        self.process_batch_result(tmp_batch, tmp_result)

    while True:
        recv_reqs = self.request_receiver.recv_requests()
        self.process_input_requests(recv_reqs)

        batch = self.get_next_batch_to_run()     # CPU: form this step's (N) batch
        self.cur_batch = batch

        if batch:                                # launch this step's forward, don't wait
            batch_result = self.run_batch(batch)
            self.result_queue.append((batch.copy(), batch_result))

        if self.last_batch:                      # while GPU runs N, CPU finishes N-1
            pop_and_process()

        self.last_batch = batch                  # back to top: next beat launches N+1
python/sglang/srt/managers/scheduler.py ::Scheduler.process_batch_resultprocess a batch's forward result: append tokens, check finished, free KV, send to detokenizer
def process_batch_result(self, batch, result):
    # in the OVERLAP loop this runs for the PREVIOUS batch while the
    # current batch is already running on the GPU:
    #  - append the sampled next tokens to each request
    #  - mark finished requests (stop / length) and free their KV
    #  - send outputs to the DetokenizerManager
    ...
📌 Key points
  • The problem is serialization: in naive event_loop_normal (Lesson 18) CPU forming/sampling/bookkeeping and the GPU forward queue up in order, so the GPU idles while the CPU works. Decode makes the GPU very fast, the CPU share dominates, and loop speed caps throughput.
  • The fix is overlap: event_loop_overlap launches step N's forward (no wait), and the CPU immediately forms batch N+1 and finishes step N−1's result (kept in result_queue / a future, one beat late). CPU scheduling hides in the GPU compute's shadow.
  • What overlaps what: step N's GPU forward ∥ step N+1's CPU batch-forming + step N−1's CPU finishing. The GPU never idles; scheduling overhead is near zero.
  • The cost is one beat: you always process results one step late (Lesson 8's throughput/latency trade-off), plus harder cross-beat state—the next batch is built before the current result exists, so SGLang carries dependencies via the FutureMap in overlap_utils.py.
  • It pairs with continuous batching: continuous batching (Lesson 5) keeps the batch always full; the overlap scheduler makes keeping it full free. This is v0.4's headline win, the "zero-overhead" cited in Lessons 1/3. Combine with CUDA Graph (Lesson 27) to shave the per-step cost further.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. In event_loop_overlap, what overlaps with what? Name precisely which part runs on the GPU and which on the CPU.
  1. Two different requests' GPU forwards run in parallel on one card; the CPU is uninvolved
  2. Step N's GPU forward (run_batch→ModelRunner.forward) runs in the same beat as the CPU forming step N+1's batch plus the CPU finishing step N−1's result (process_batch_result): after launching this step's forward without waiting, the CPU immediately forms the next batch and pops the previous step's result from result_queue to finish it
  3. This step's CPU finishing overlaps with this step's own GPU forward (parallel within the same step)
  4. The GPU forward overlaps with GPU sampling, with no CPU work at all
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. The scheduler 'decides, never computes': forming/sampling/finishing are light CPU work, while only run_batch burns GPU. The overlap loop launches step N's forward and appends to result_queue without blocking; while the GPU computes N, the CPU forms N+1's batch and poplefts N−1's result to finish it. So each beat's CPU time hides in the same beat's GPU compute shadow.
2. Why does this overlap hide scheduling overhead down to near zero, earning the name 'zero-overhead'?
  1. Because overlap makes the CPU scheduling code itself run faster
  2. Because the CPU's forming/sampling/bookkeeping time falls inside the 'shadow' of that beat's GPU forward and is fully masked—the GPU never idles waiting on the CPU, so the CPU cost leaves the critical path, presenting as near-zero scheduling overhead with the GPU saturated
  3. Because it deletes the batch-forming and sampling steps entirely
  4. Because the forward is moved onto the CPU, eliminating the GPU
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. 'Zero-overhead' isn't cost-free; it moves the cost somewhere that doesn't dent throughput. In the serial version the GPU idles while the CPU works, and that idle caps throughput; overlap puts CPU scheduling and the GPU forward in the same beat, masking CPU time behind GPU compute and saturating utilization. The win is biggest on decode, where the GPU is very fast and the CPU share is large.
3. What is the overlap scheduler's main cost, and why is a result_queue needed?
  1. The cost is one extra beat of latency—you always process the previous step's result, forever one step behind real time (Lesson 8's throughput/latency trade-off), plus harder cross-beat state; the result_queue holds 'launched but not yet finished' batches, deferring the finish one beat so it's popleft-ed once the GPU is done
  2. The cost is doubled VRAM; the result_queue caches KV
  3. The cost is lower throughput; the result_queue throttles intake
  4. There is no cost; the result_queue is just a logging buffer
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. In source, right after run_batch we result_queue.append((batch.copy(), result)) without blocking, and only the next beat poplefts it for process_batch_result—so finishing always lags one beat, hence +1 latency. The next batch is built before the current result exists, giving sampled-token / KV-bookkeeping cross-beat dependencies threaded via the FutureMap in overlap_utils.py. Cases needing the prior result first fall back to no-overlap temporarily.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • Using the 'relay race / two-stage assembly line' analogy, walk through one step of event_loop_overlap's staggered pipeline. Cover each: (1) launch step N's forward (run_batch without waiting, push (batch.copy(), result) onto result_queue); (2) while the GPU computes N, the CPU forms step N+1's batch; (3) pop_and_process finishes step N−1 (process_batch_result: append tokens, detect finished, free KV, send to detok, Lesson 17). Explain why being 'one beat behind' is both the cost and the source of 'zero-overhead,' and, contrasting naive event_loop_normal (Lesson 18), why the GPU never idles.
  • Argue that 'the overlap scheduler pairs perfectly with continuous batching': continuous batching (Lesson 5) keeps the batch always full, while the overlap scheduler makes keeping it full free. Explain which half of the throughput problem each solves, why the overlap win is largest in decode-heavy regimes (tie to Lesson 8 throughput/latency and Lesson 24 model forward), and how it stacks with CUDA Graph (Lesson 27) to further thin the per-step cost. Then discuss: under what workloads or latency-sensitive scenarios might the +1-beat latency be an unacceptable trade-off?

Chunked prefill

Last lesson we saw the scheduler's two-step move: order first, then throttle. This lesson zooms in on the trickiest throttling case: what happens when the head of the queue is a huge prompt (say a 32k-token document)? If you let it prefill entirely in ONE step, that step becomes a "whale"—it monopolizes the whole GPU for tens of milliseconds, and every other request that is decoding behind it stalls completely: nobody's tokens come out, and latency spikes (TTFT/ITL blow up, Lesson 8). Chunked prefill cuts that whale into fixed-size token chunks, spread over several steps, each step nibbling one small bite while mixed in the same batch with everyone else's decode. No step is enormous; everyone's tokens keep flowing. Remember one line: never let one long request blow up a step—chunk it and feed it slowly, mixed with decode.

🔌 Real-world analogy
Picture this step's GPU as a lively dinner table. Everyone takes turns talking (each request's decode emits a token in turn), conversation flowing. Then a giant meal arrives (a 32k-token prompt). The dumb move: have one person stuff the whole plate in their mouth at once—now they chew head-down for tens of seconds, during which nobody can get a word in; one person choked the whole table's chat. The smart move is to eat the giant meal in small bites while still holding the conversation: each round you swallow just one bite (one chunk), chew, say a line, then take the next bite. The meal still gets finished, and the table's chat (everyone else's decode) never stops. The key discipline: each bite has a fixed size cap—nobody is ever allowed to cram their mouth full and block everyone's turn. The cost is real too: the big meal takes several rounds to finish (a few extra steps, and its own first byte is slower), but in exchange the whole table stays smooth—nobody gets choked off.
🌍 Big picture
In one line: split a big prefill into many small per-step chunks, mix them with decode, and trade a few extra steps for globally smooth latency. Who does the cutting? The very PrefillAdder from last lesson (Lesson 20). It holds a token budget (the server arg chunked_prefill_size sets how many prefill tokens fit per step) and, inside add_one_req, tries each request: if the whole thing fits, commit it; if it doesn't, slice off only the part that fits and leave the rest for the next step. So a 32k prompt gets cut into several chunks across consecutive steps—its KV cache (Lesson 4) fills chunk by chunk, and only after the last chunk does that request switch into decode and emit its first token. And every such step runs a mixed batch: some requests prefilling a chunk, others decoding, with the event loop (Lesson 18) advancing as usual. Pull back: chunked prefill and last lesson's throttling are two sides of one coin—throttling guarantees "don't eat too much in one step," chunking guarantees "what doesn't fit isn't choked down whole either; slice it and take it slow." It tames an extreme that would otherwise spike latency into a stream of steady small steps—the key to SGLang staying smooth under mixed long/short traffic.

The problem: one whale clogs the whole pool

First, what happens without chunking. Continuous batching (Lesson 5) rests on a happy premise: "every step is fast, and the batch advances together." But the moment a 32k-token prefill jumps to the head and you make it finish in one step, that step's forward compute explodes tens of times over— the GPU is monopolized for tens of milliseconds, during which every other decode request in the batch just waits: they should emit one token per step, but now they stall alongside the whale. To the user this reads as a streaming answer that suddenly freezes (ITL spike), plus longer first-token latency for new requests (TTFT spike, Lesson 8). One overlong request ruined an entire batch's experience. This is not a "won't fit in memory" problem—KV slots may be plenty; it is a "too much compute in one step" problem, congestion along the time axis. Chunked prefill exists precisely to flatten this "one big fish stops the whole pool" spike.

❌ One-step full prefill (spike)

32k tokens finish in a single step: that step is enormous, monopolizing the GPU for tens of ms. Every other request's decode in the batch stalls, no tokens emitted. TTFT/ITL spike instantly (Lesson 8); users see output freeze. One whale clogs the pool.

✅ Chunked prefill (smooth)

The 32k is cut into fixed-size chunks over several steps. Each step computes one chunk + is mixed with everyone's decode; no step is huge. Everyone's tokens keep flowing, latency steady. A few extra steps buy global smoothness and better goodput-under-SLA.

Fig 1 · Chunk a long prefill, interleave with decode — without chunking one 32k prefill hogs a step and every other request's decode stalls (red dashed region); with chunking it is split into fixed-size chunks, with ongoing decode (blue bars) slipped between them, and the timeline turns smooth again.

How to cut: split 32k into multi-step chunks

The mechanism is plain and effective: each step, PrefillAdder carries a fixed chunk-token budget (rem_chunk_tokens, derived from chunked_prefill_size). When a request's pending input_tokens is less than or equal to the remaining chunk budget, the whole thing is committed at once via the non-chunked path; once it exceeds it, the chunked branch kicks in: take only trunc_len tokens (floored to page_size for page alignment), mark that slice as this step's extend range (set_extend_range), and leave the rest. The request is recorded as new_chunked_req, and next step it returns with its filled prefix and slices the next chunk from the cut point. Round after round, the prompt's KV cache (Lesson 4) accumulates chunk by chunk, until the last chunk is filled and it finally leaves prefill and enters decode. The diagram below traces a 32k prompt being chunked across consecutive steps.

1

Step 1: chunk #1

Take the 32k prompt at the head; input_tokens > rem_chunk_tokens → slice only trunc_len (e.g. 8k, page-aligned). Fill the first 8k of KV, running mixed with others' decode.

2

Step 2: chunk #2

The same request returns as new_chunked_req, slicing the next 8k from offset 8k. Another bounded small step; decode keeps flowing.

3

Steps 3…N: continue

Chunk after chunk fills the KV (Lesson 4). No step is large, none monopolizes the GPU—the spike is fully flattened.

4

Last chunk: enter decode

When the remainder fits in one step, the non-chunked path commits it whole. The prompt is now fully prefilled, and the request emits its first token.

Mixed batch: prefill and decode in the same step

The reason chunking protects others' latency is the mixed batch: the prefill chunk that was sliced off does not get a step to itself; it shares one batch with a crowd of decoding requests in the same forward pass. So each step's work = one bounded prefill chunk + one decode token from every running request—controlled in size, steady in rhythm. This also answers "why not just queue the long request last"—that would starve it, with unbounded first-token latency; chunking lets it slice-and-mix, making steady prefill progress without stealing everyone's throughput. The diagram below shows the makeup of one mixed-batch step: the highlighted cell is the long request's one prefill chunk, the rest are each request's decode. The chunk's size (the highlighted cell's "width") is set by chunked_prefill_size—the very knob between smoothness and step count.

One step's mixed batch: 1 prefill chunk (highlighted) + several decodes in the same forward
memberslong req · chunk#kR2 decodeR3 decodeR4 decodeR5 decodemixed batch
this stepfill 8k KVemit 1 tokemit 1 tokemit 1 tokemit 1 tokbounded + flowing
next stepchunk#k+1keep decodingkeep decodingkeep decodingkeep decodingsteady rhythm
Fig 2 · TTFT/ITL jitter: before vs after chunking — before chunking other requests' inter-token latency spikes (one big prefill blows up the step, the ITL/TTFT spike of Lesson 8); after chunking each step's prefill is bounded, the spike is capped, and the curve stays smooth.

The trade-off: chunk size is the knob

Chunking isn't free: it splits a prefill across steps, so total step count rises, and the whale's own TTFT is spread over several steps—its first byte is a bit slower. What you buy is smooth latency for everyone and higher goodput-under-SLA—in real mixed long/short workloads this is almost always worth it. And chunked_prefill_size is the knob: smaller means lighter prefill per step and smoother decode, but more steps, a slower whale first-byte, and more fixed overhead amortized; larger means fewer steps and a faster whale finish, but heavier steps and the spike risk creeping back (back to Lesson 8's throughput/latency trade-off). There's no universal optimum—it depends on your latency SLA and your long/short mix. The table lays the two ends side by side.

chunk sizeprefill per stepdecode smoothnesstotal steps / whale TTFTfits
smallerlight, boundedvery smooth, tiny spikesmore steps, slower whale first bytelatency-sensitive, decode-heavy
largerheavy, near-wholeworse, spike returnsfewer steps, faster whale finishthroughput-first, long-prompt-heavy
chunking offwhole in one biteworst, one step stalls allfewest steps, but latency blows uprarely advised (fine only for short prompts)

Read the source: the chunk decision in add_one_req

Chunking lives in schedule_policy.py's PrefillAdder.add_one_req. It first computes how many tokens the request needs, runs the token/memory budget checks, then an if / else decides: commit the whole thing if it fits, otherwise take the chunked prefill branch—floor trunc_len to page_size, commit only that slice, and record the request as new_chunked_req to continue next step. The most telling lines follow.

schedule_policy.py ::PrefillAdder.add_one_reqchunk decision
# whole fits (input_tokens ≤ remaining chunk budget) → commit at once, no chunking
elif self.rem_chunk_tokens is None or input_tokens <= self.rem_chunk_tokens:
    req.set_extend_range(# commit the whole prompt this step
        len(req.prefix_indices), len(req.full_untruncated_fill_ids))
    self.can_run_list.append(req)
else:                                            # doesn't fit → chunked prefill
    trunc_len = self.rem_chunk_tokens // self.page_size * self.page_size  # page-aligned cut
    if trunc_len <= 0:
        return AddReqResult.OTHER
    req.set_extend_range(# compute only the first trunc_len tokens; rest next step
        len(req.prefix_indices), len(req.prefix_indices) + trunc_len)
    self.can_run_list.append(req)
    self.new_chunked_req = req                    # remember it; slice the next chunk next step
python/sglang/srt/managers/scheduler.py ::Scheduler.get_new_batch_prefillbuild a prefill batch within a token budget: chunk long prompts, don't starve decode
def get_new_batch_prefill(self):
    # build the next PREFILL batch within a token budget. A long prompt is
    # admitted in CHUNKS (chunked_prefill_size) so it doesn't monopolize one
    # step and stall ongoing decode; the rest of its prefill resumes later.
    ...
    return new_batch   # Optional[ScheduleBatch]

A concrete example: with the default --chunked-prefill-size 4096, a 32768-token prompt is cut into 8 chunks (32768 ÷ 4096 = 8 steps), each step computing only 4096 prefill tokens mixed with other requests' decode; turn the knob to --chunked-prefill-size 2048 and the same prompt becomes 16 chunks—smoother, but twice the steps and a slower whale first byte.

📌 Key points
  • The problem: a huge prompt (e.g. 32k) prefilled whole in one step monopolizes the GPU and stalls every decode in the batch, causing TTFT/ITL latency spikes (Lesson 8). One whale clogs the pool.
  • The fix: split the big prefill into fixed-size token chunks (set by chunked_prefill_size) across several steps, mixed in the same batch with others' decode—no step is huge, everyone's tokens keep flowing.
  • Who cuts: PrefillAdder (Lesson 20) enforces the per-step token budget in add_one_req, clamping a non-fitting request to the remaining budget (trunc_len, page-aligned) and recording new_chunked_req for next step; KV fills chunk by chunk (Lesson 4), and only after the last chunk does it enter decode.
  • Trade-off & knob: chunking costs more steps and a thinner whale TTFT, buying smooth global latency and higher goodput-under-SLA. chunked_prefill_size smaller = smoother but more steps; larger = fewer steps but the spike returns (Lesson 8's trade-off).
  • One-line close: never let one long request blow up a step—chunk it and feed it slowly, mixed with decode; spend a few extra steps to keep the whole table smooth. This is also the seed of the motivation for PD disaggregation (Lesson 45), which splits prefill and decode onto separate instances entirely.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. Without chunking, why does a 32k-token huge prompt become a big problem?
  1. Because a 32k-token KV cache can never fit in VRAM and must OOM
  2. Because the scheduler cannot order requests longer than 4k tokens
  3. Because if it is prefilled whole in one step, that step's forward compute explodes and monopolizes the GPU for tens of ms; every decoding request in the batch stalls, no tokens emitted, so TTFT/ITL spike (Lesson 8)—one whale clogs the whole pool
  4. Because a long prompt breaks sampling temperature, producing garbage output
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. It's congestion along the time axis, not a memory problem—KV slots may be plenty, but 'too much compute in one step' lets one request monopolize the GPU while everyone else's decode waits. Users see streaming freeze (ITL spike) and slower first tokens (TTFT spike). Chunking exists to flatten exactly this spike.
2. How does chunked prefill actually work so it advances the long request without stalling others?
  1. Give the long request its own dedicated GPU, physically isolated from decode
  2. Compress the prompt to under 4k tokens then prefill it all at once
  3. Move the long request to the very end of the queue and prefill it whole only after all decodes finish
  4. Split the big prefill into fixed-size token chunks (set by chunked_prefill_size) across several steps, forwarded in the same batch mixed with others' decode; KV fills chunk by chunk, and only after the last chunk does the request enter decode
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. Two keys: (1) chunking bounds prefill per step so no step is huge; (2) the mixed batch forwards that one chunk together with a crowd of decodes, so each step = one bounded prefill chunk + one token from everyone—controlled and steady. Queuing it last would starve the long request with unbounded first-token latency, so that's no good.
3. Tuning the chunked_prefill_size knob smaller vs larger means what trade-off?
  1. Smaller: lighter prefill per step and smoother decode, but more total steps and a slower whale first byte; larger: fewer steps and a faster whale finish, but heavier steps and the spike risk returns (back to Lesson 8's throughput/latency trade-off)
  2. It only controls logging frequency and has no performance effect
  3. Smaller raises throughput, larger lowers it, with no latency effect
  4. It sets the KV cache page size, where larger saves more VRAM
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. chunked_prefill_size sets how many prefill tokens fit per step—the very knob between smoothness and step count. There's no universal optimum: go smaller for latency-sensitive, decode-heavy loads; larger for throughput-first, long-prompt-heavy ones. It controls the bounded per-step prefill cap, not the VRAM page size or logging.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • Using the 'eating a giant meal while still chatting' analogy, fully explain chunked prefill. Cover each: (1) the problem—a 32k prompt prefilled whole in one step monopolizes the GPU and stalls every decode in the batch, causing TTFT/ITL spikes (Lesson 8); (2) the fix—PrefillAdder (Lesson 20) enforces the per-step token budget in add_one_req, clamping a non-fitting request to the remaining budget (trunc_len, page-aligned), recording new_chunked_req for next step, filling KV chunk by chunk (Lesson 4), entering decode only after the last chunk; (3) the mixed batch—that one chunk is forwarded alongside others' decode, the event loop (Lesson 18) advancing as usual.
  • Discuss chunked prefill's trade-off and its place in the system. Chunking costs a few extra steps and thins the whale's own TTFT, but buys smooth global latency and higher goodput-under-SLA—explain why this is usually worth it in real mixed long/short workloads, the costs of chunked_prefill_size being too small vs too large (tie to Lesson 8), and why mixing prefill and decode in one step still has tension, motivating PD disaggregation (Lesson 45) that splits the two onto separate instances entirely.

DP controller & PP scheduling

For five lessons we watched one scheduler process beat on one card: receive, batch, forward, finish. But real deployments have 8 cards per box and hundreds per cluster— a model may be too big for one card, and traffic may be too heavy for one replica. Closing Part 5, we pull the camera back to the multi-process, multi-card scale and look at two parallelisms that change the control flow: data parallel (DP) (replicate the whole runtime N times and fan requests out) and pipeline parallel (PP) (split the model's layers into stages and keep multiple micro-batches flowing across them). Note: what they change is not how the matrices compute, but who dispatches and how many micro-batches are in flight—exactly the scheduler's concern.

🔌 Analogy
Think of DP as multiple identical checkout lanes in a store: each lane is fully equipped (its own cashier, scanner, register), and a greeter at the door routes each customer to a lane; lanes don't interfere, and the more you open, the more customers you ring up per minute. Think of PP as one long conveyor with stations: each station does just one step (label → box → seal), and many items ride at once, each at a different station, so no station sits idle. DP is "replicate whole lanes for throughput", PP is "split one job into a relay so everyone stays busy"—one handles dispatch, one handles relay.
🌍 The big picture
In one line: DP replicates "the whole model" and fans requests out to many independent replicas; PP splits "the model's layers" and pipelines multiple micro-batches across stages. DP's conductor is the DataParallelController—it does no forward itself, only dispatching incoming requests round-robin or load-aware to N full runtimes (each replica has its own scheduler + TP workers + KV cache + a full copy of the model weights). PP's executor is SchedulerPPMixin.event_loop_pp—it reshapes Lesson 18's single-stage heartbeat into a pipelined loop, so stage1 processes micro-batch B while stage2 processes micro-batch A, amortizing the fill/drain "bubble". Layer on tensor parallel (TP) (split each layer's matrices, deep dive Lesson 46) and a big deployment is TP × PP × DP stitched in three dimensions.

Data parallel (DP): replicate the whole runtime, fan requests out

DP's idea is the plainest: if one replica can't keep up with traffic, run N replicas. The key is that each replica is a complete engine— not sharing some weights, but each holding a full model copy, each running a Lesson-18 event loop, each managing its own KV ledger. Replicas are request-isolated: customer A on replica 1, customer B on replica 2, never interacting. The only coordination needed is that dispatcher at the door— the DataParallelController. It catches all incoming requests up front, picks a replica by policy (round-robin round_robin_scheduler, or load-aware by in-flight requests/tokens), forwards the request as-is, and never touches the GPU.

DataParallelController
round-robin / load-aware
round_robin_scheduler
→
Replica 1 (full runtime)
scheduler+TP+KV
a full model
→
Replica 2 (full runtime)
scheduler+TP+KV
a full model
→
Replica N (full runtime)
scheduler+TP+KV
a full model
Fig 23A · DP: requests split across replicas — the request stream enters the dispatch-only DataParallelController, fanned out in round_robin order 0→1→2→0 to N full replicas (each with its own scheduler+TP+KV+a full model); replicas 0/1/2 each hold different requests (r3 wraps back to replica 0), and the controller never touches the GPU.

Two concrete numbers to ground this. --dp-size 4 spins up 4 full replicas (replica 0/1/2/3), and the controller hands requests out in 0→1→2→3→0 order, round and round; while --pp-size 2 cuts the model's layers into 2 stages relayed across 2 cards. The former scales throughput, the latter scales capacity.

Read three things off this figure. First, the controller sits before all replicas, the first fork a request hits, making only the "to whom" decision. Second, each replica is self-contained—inside it is everything from the last five lessons: an event_loop_normal, a prefill/decode trade-off, an independent KV ledger. Third, DP is a throughput multiplier: N replicas can scale whole-box throughput N-fold, since requests are spread and don't contend. A caveat: this is request-level DP; in MoE models there is finer-grained attention DP / expert DP, with more nuanced control flow and communication, deferred to Lessons 46/47 and not covered here.

Pipeline parallel (PP): split layers into stages, keep micro-batches in flight

When a model is too big for one card, DP replication is hopeless—you can't even fit one copy, let alone N. Now use PP: cut the model's layers vertically into a few stages, layers 1–10 on GPU0, 11–20 on GPU1, 21–30 on GPU2…, so each card holds one stage. A batch's forward becomes a relay: data passes stage1, hands activations to stage2, then stage3… The catch: with only one batch in flight, while stage2 works, stage1/stage3 sit idle waiting, and GPU utilization is dismal—this is the pipeline bubble. The fix is to split a big batch into multiple micro-batches and run them staggered, in flight together.

t1

Fill

micro-batch A enters stage1; stage2, stage3 still empty—this tick is part of the bubble, only one stage busy.

t2

Ramping

A advances to stage2 while micro-batch B enters stage1; two stages busy, the pipe begins to fill.

t3

Full (steady state)

A in stage3, B in stage2, C enters stage1—all three busy, bubble amortized; this is where PP wants to live.

t4

Drain

After the last micro-batch is launched, A has emitted and B/C walk off the tail stages; a tail bubble reappears. More micro-batches ⇒ higher full-load fraction, smaller bubble.

Fig 23B · PP: layers as stages, micro-batches pipelined — model layers split into S0/S1/S2 on three cards, micro-batches mb0/mb1/mb2 flowing diagonally and staggered; t0–t1 are the fill bubble with only some stages busy, t2 is the all-stages-busy steady state, and t3–t4 are the tail drain bubble. More micro-batches ⇒ higher steady-state fraction, thinner bubble.

Grasp this staggered figure and you've got PP's soul: at any instant, different stages process different micro-batches. Precisely because multiple micro-batches are in flight, each stage's GPU never idles waiting on the previous—this is why event_loop_pp must be more complex than the normal loop: it tracks several micro-batches at different stages, manages inter-stage activation send/recv, and coordinates "when to launch the next micro-batch". A plain single-stage loop watches one batch per tick; the PP loop watches a string of them. The fill and drain ends carry unavoidable bubbles, but as long as steady state is long enough (micro-batches ≫ stages), the bubble fraction approaches zero.

How the three compose: TP × PP × DP

DP, PP, and the TP (tensor parallel) that Lesson 46 details are three orthogonal dimensions you can stack. The fastest way to tell them apart is to ask: "what does it replicate, what does it split?"

DimensionWhat it does to the modelGranularity of split/replicateWho orchestrates
DP data parallelReplicate the whole model, fan requests to replicaswhole runtime (replica level)DataParallelController
PP pipeline parallelSplit layers into stages across cards, micro-batch relaylayers (stage level)event_loop_pp (in scheduler)
TP tensor parallelSplit each layer's matrices, many cards compute one layera single layer's weight matrixinside model forward (Lessons 24/46)

Picture them stacked: TP within a layer (many cards co-compute one layer's matmul), PP across layers (different stages on different cards), DP across whole replicas (replicate the box and fan out). A typical large cluster might be "TP=8, PP=2, DP=4"—each replica uses 16 cards (8-way TP × 2 PP stages) to hold one copy of a big model, then 4 replicas to carry traffic, 64 cards in all. And what the scheduler orchestrates here is DP (via the controller's dispatch) and PP (via the pp loop advancing micro-batches)—the two dimensions that change control flow; TP hides inside the model forward, largely transparent to the scheduler—the scheduler hands the batch to ModelRunner and TP's multi-card cooperation happens automatically inside forward (Lesson 24). That's why this lesson belongs in Part 5: DP and PP are scheduling problems, TP is a compute problem.

When to use DP vs PP

They solve different bottlenecks—don't conflate them:

DP: for throughput (model fits)

The premise is one copy already fits the existing cards. When traffic is high and a single replica queues badly, replicate N copies and let the controller spread requests, scaling throughput near-linearly. The cost is VRAM multiplied (a full weight set per replica). It cannot help you fit a model that doesn't fit—replication just makes "doesn't fit" into "doesn't fit N times".

PP: for capacity (model too big)

The premise is the model is too big for one card (even one TP group). Split layers into stages across cards so each holds only one stage of weights, turning "doesn't fit" into "fits". The cost is fill/drain bubbles and inter-stage communication, amortized by many micro-batches. It does not directly improve single-request latency—mainly it makes a huge model runnable.

In practice the two often appear together: first use PP (and TP) to fit one big model into a card group, then use DP to replicate that ready-made replica to carry traffic. So by now you should be able to translate this into concrete actions—"make it fit" via splitting (PP/TP), "keep it fed" via replication (DP).

Real code: how the controller round-robins dispatch

Below is the core round-robin dispatch in DataParallelController. Note how light it is— it keeps a round_robin_counter, finds the next ready worker, sock_sends the request there, and advances the pointer modulo the worker count. It never touches the GPU, making only the "to whom" decision.

python/sglang/srt/managers/data_parallel_controller.py ::DataParallelControllerDP controller: round-robin to the next ready replica
def round_robin_scheduler(self, req: Req):
    # Direct-route if a DP rank is pinned, else round-robin
    if self.maybe_external_dp_rank_routing(req):
        return

    while True:
        if self.status[self.round_robin_counter]:
            # This worker is ready: send the request
            sock_send(self.workers[self.round_robin_counter], req)
            self.round_robin_counter = (self.round_robin_counter + 1) % len(
                self.workers
            )
            break
        # Otherwise skip it, look at the next
        self.round_robin_counter = (self.round_robin_counter + 1) % len(
            self.workers
        )
python/sglang/srt/managers/data_parallel_controller.py ::DataParallelController.round_robin_schedulerround-robin: hand each request to the next DP worker
def round_robin_scheduler(self, req):
    # send this request to the next DP worker in rotation, then advance.
    self.workers[self.round_robin_counter].send_pyobj(req)
    self.round_robin_counter = (self.round_robin_counter + 1) % len(self.workers)

Align this with the fan-out figure: self.workers are the N replicas' sockets, round_robin_counter is the greeter's "which lane is next" counter, self.status marks which replicas are ready (skip the ones that aren't). sock_send delivers the request as-is to the chosen replica—after that the request's fate is entirely up to that replica's internal scheduler, and the controller immediately turns to the next. Not one line of matmul, confirming "the controller only decides dispatch, never computes". Load-aware policy (pick the least-busy replica by in-flight requests/tokens) just swaps "advance the pointer" for "consult the ledger", equally lightweight.

📌 Key points
  • DP (data parallel): replicate the whole runtime into N independent replicas (each with scheduler+TP+KV+a full model), with DataParallelController fanning out requests round-robin/load-aware; a throughput multiplier, request-isolated, replicas don't interact.
  • PP (pipeline parallel): split the model's layers into stages across cards; event_loop_pp keeps multiple micro-batches flowing across stages (stage1 on B while stage2 on A), amortizing the fill/drain bubble via micro-batch count; born to fit a huge model.
  • TP × PP × DP are three orthogonal dims: TP splits matrices within a layer, PP splits stages across layers, DP replicates whole replicas. The scheduler orchestrates DP (controller) and PP (pp loop); TP hides in the model forward, transparent to the scheduler.
  • Selection rule: model too big ⇒ PP/TP (split, "make it fit"); traffic too heavy ⇒ DP (replicate, "keep it fed"); big deployments stack all three. Deep dives: Lesson 46 (TP/PP/EP/DP), Lesson 47 (EPLB), Lesson 24 (forward), Lesson 18 (event loop), Lesson 2 (3-process model).

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. In data parallel (DP), what role does each replica vs the DataParallelController play?
  1. Each replica holds only some layers, and the controller passes activations between them
  2. The controller splits each request into pieces, one per replica, then stitches them back
  3. Each replica is a full runtime (its own scheduler+TP worker+KV cache+a full model copy), request-isolated and non-interacting; the controller does no forward, only fanning out incoming requests round-robin/load-aware to a ready replica
  4. Replicas share one set of weights, and the controller merges their outputs
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. DP replicates the whole runtime: each replica holds a full model, runs its own event loop, manages its own KV ledger, and whatever replica a request lands on handles it end-to-end. The controller does one ultra-light thing—'to whom'—never touching the GPU. Splitting layers/passing activations is PP; sharing weights and merging outputs is not how DP works.
2. Why does pipeline parallel (PP) keep multiple micro-batches in flight instead of running one batch at a time?
  1. Because multiple micro-batches can share one KV cache to save VRAM
  2. Because layers are split into stages across cards; with one batch, while one stage works the others idle (the pipeline bubble). Staggering multiple micro-batches lets stage1 run B while stage2 runs A, so all stages stay busy and the bubble is amortized
  3. Because a single batch cannot be sampled and must be split into micro-batches
  4. Because it skips inter-stage communication and avoids passing activations
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. PP's bottleneck is the fill/drain bubble: in relay-style forward, with one batch some stage always idles. Staggered micro-batches drive the pipe into steady-state full load (different stages on different micro-batches); more micro-batches ⇒ higher full-load fraction ⇒ bubble approaching zero. That's why event_loop_pp must be more complex than the normal loop—it tracks a string of micro-batches at different stages.
3. Distinguishing TP, PP, DP in one line, which statement is most accurate?
  1. All three split the model's layers, differing only in stage count
  2. All three are orchestrated uniformly by the DataParallelController
  3. TP splits matrices within a layer, PP splits stages across layers, DP replicates whole replicas; the scheduler orchestrates DP (controller dispatch) and PP (pp loop), while TP hides inside the model forward, largely transparent to the scheduler
  4. TP replicates the whole model, PP replicates the whole model, DP splits matrices
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. Three orthogonal dims, told apart by 'what it replicates/splits': DP=whole-model replicate (replica level), PP=layers into stages (stage level), TP=split a single layer's matrices. Big deployments stack TP×PP×DP. The key is separating the scheduling problem (DP/PP change control flow, hence Part 5) from the compute problem (TP inside forward, Lessons 24/46).
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • Using the 'supermarket checkout lanes vs conveyor stations' analogy, fully explain DP vs PP. Cover each: (1) DP—why it replicates the whole runtime (each replica with its own scheduler+TP+KV+a full model), how DataParallelController round-robins/fans out requests via round_robin_scheduler without touching the GPU, and why it is a throughput multiplier yet can't rescue a model that 'doesn't fit'; (2) PP—why splitting layers into stages fits a huge model, why event_loop_pp keeps multiple micro-batches staggered in flight, and where the fill/drain bubble comes from and how micro-batch count amortizes it.
  • Discuss how TP × PP × DP compose in a large cluster, and why this lesson belongs to 'scheduling'. Explain: (1) for a 'TP=8, PP=2, DP=4' deployment, what each dimension splits/replicates and how the total card count is computed; (2) why the scheduler orchestrates DP (controller dispatch) and PP (the pp loop advancing micro-batches) while TP is transparent to the scheduler (hidden in forward, Lessons 24/46); (3) from this, state the selection rule—which parallelism to reach for when the model is too big vs traffic too heavy—and point to the deep dives (Lesson 46 TP/PP/EP/DP, Lesson 47 EPLB).

ModelRunner & ForwardBatch

Part 5 was all about how the scheduler decides — receive, batch, set policy. But it only decides; it never computes. The thing that actually turns a batch of tokens into logits on the GPU is this lesson's star, ModelRunner. It is the door where "decide" becomes "compute": Part 5 was control flow; from here we step into the real math.

🔌 Analogy
Think of ModelRunner as the machine operator on the floor: the scheduler (foreman) hands over a work order (ForwardBatch: which tokens, prefill or decode, where the KV lives); the operator runs the machine (model.forward), reads the gauge (logits), and passes it to the inspector (Sampler) who picks this step's output (the next token). The foreman never touches the machine — only the operator does.
🌍 The big picture
Each TP rank has one ModelRunner (owned by the TpWorker); it holds the loaded model (Lessons 25/26) and the KV pool (Lesson 30). One forward(forward_batch) runs a batch into logits, and sample() hands them to the Sampler (Lesson 28) for the token. A ForwardBatch is the GPU view of a ScheduleBatch (Lesson 19) — the same batch, re-expressed as tensors and metadata the GPU understands.

After run_batch: who handed this batch to ModelRunner

Let's complete the call chain. The scheduler's main loop (Lesson 18) calls run_batch every step, but run_batch itself is thin: it passes the freshly assembled ScheduleBatch to the TpWorker, which hands it straight to the one ModelRunner it owns. The chain is layered this deep because each layer watches exactly one thing — the scheduler cares about "which batch to run this step", the TpWorker cares about "my rank's boundary and cross-card communication", and the ModelRunner cares about "actually computing the tensors on this card". Clean, non-overlapping responsibilities are precisely what lets thousands of contributors maintain this code together.

The chain also hides a subtle but fatal constraint: in an 8-way TP deployment, the 8 TpWorkers' ModelRunners must run the same batch in strict lockstep. Each computes a slice of the model (weights split row/column-wise, Lessons 25/46), and mid-layer they stitch attention outputs and residuals back together via all-reduce / all-gather — if any rank steps even half a beat early or late, the collective deadlocks the whole group. That is why, before the forward, a ScheduleBatch's contents must be byte-for-byte identical across all ranks: same tokens, same shapes, same forward_mode. Once you see this, Lesson 18's insistence that scheduling be deterministic and reproducible is no fussiness — it is the hard prerequisite for a distributed forward that doesn't hang.

From ScheduleBatch to ForwardBatch: a GPU pair of glasses

The scheduler hands over a ScheduleBatch (a set of Reqs, Lesson 19). ModelRunner first translates it into a ForwardBatch — exactly what a GPU forward needs: input_ids (the tokens to feed this step), positions (each token's position, for RoPE), forward_mode (EXTEND/prefill vs DECODE), attention metadata (which backend, which KV slots to read/write, Lesson 33), and pointers into the KV pool. In one line: ScheduleBatch records "who needs computing"; ForwardBatch records "how the GPU computes this step".

ScheduleBatch
a batch of Reqs (L19)
→
ForwardBatch
GPU view: ids/positions/mode/KV
→
model.forward
embed→layers→norm→lm_head
→
logits
next-token dist per request
→
Sampler
pick the next token (L28)
ForwardBatch fieldwhat it isused by
input_idstoken ids to forward this stepthe embedding
positionseach token's absolute positionRoPE / pos encoding (L36)
forward_modeEXTEND (prefill) / DECODEpicks the forward path
attn metadataattention backend + KV slotsattention kernel (L33)
out_cache_locwhere new K/V are writtenKV cache pool (L30)
Fig 24·1 · one forward — ModelRunner takes a ForwardBatch (input_ids / positions / seq_lens / out_cache_loc), runs left-to-right through the model's layer stack (embed → N decoder layers → norm + lm_head), and finally produces next-token logits.

These fields aren't invented out of thin air; they map directly onto the ForwardBatch dataclass in the source — every input of a single forward pass, packed into one struct:

python/sglang/srt/model_executor/forward_batch_info.py ::ForwardBatchone struct holding every input of a single forward pass
class ForwardBatch:
    # every input of ONE forward pass, packed together.
    forward_mode: ForwardMode      # EXTEND (prefill) or DECODE
    batch_size: int
    input_ids: torch.Tensor        # tokens to run this step
    req_pool_indices: torch.Tensor # which requests (rows in req_to_token)
    seq_lens: torch.Tensor         # per-request context length
    out_cache_loc: torch.Tensor    # where to write the new KV
    ...

Read that table one level deeper and it gets tastier. positions is no decoration: it feeds straight into RoPE and sets each token's rotation phase, so the moment prefill or decode gets an absolute position wrong, attention "misjudges" near-vs-far relationships and the output goes haywire — that's exactly why chunked prefill (Lesson 22) must carefully preserve positional continuity. forward_mode is the master switch; one flip decides the whole path: variable-length prefill kernel vs regular decode kernel, whether a CUDA graph applies, which mask the attention backend uses. The attention metadata translates "the logical k-th token" into "which physical page and slot in the KV pool", and together with out_cache_loc it tells the kernel exactly where to write the new K/V and where to read the historical K/V. In short, these ForwardBatch fields together form a GPU addressing table that every read and write in the forward follows; how well they are organized directly decides whether the downstream attention kernel (Lesson 33) and CUDA graph (Lesson 27) run fast and stable.

ModelRunner.forward: how one forward goes

It forks two ways: if a CUDA graph (Lesson 27) applies, replay a recorded graph; otherwise run the batch eagerly. The key is can_run_graph — usually only decode steps (regular shapes, small batch) take graph replay, while prefill (variable lengths) mostly runs eager (or a piecewise prefill graph). Here is the real skeleton of _forward_raw:

python/sglang/srt/model_executor/model_runner.py ::ModelRunner.forwardthe forward dispatch
def _forward_raw(self, forward_batch, pp_proxy_tensors, ...):
    # Can we replay the decode CUDA graph: mode matches + graph exists + shape fits
    can_run_graph = (forward_batch.forward_mode.is_cuda_graph()
                     and self.decode_cuda_graph_runner
                     and self.decode_cuda_graph_runner.can_run_graph(forward_batch))
    if can_run_graph:                          # decode: replay the recorded graph (L27)
        ret = self.decode_cuda_graph_runner.execute(forward_batch, ...)
        return ModelRunnerOutput(logits_output=ret, can_run_graph=True)
    self._prepare_eager_forward_batch(forward_batch)  # eager path: DP/padding normalize
    if forward_batch.forward_mode.is_extend(...):   # prefill: run the batch
        ...                                       # else eager decode fallback

Either path ends in model.forward(input_ids, positions, forward_batch): embed → a stack of decoder layers (attention + MLP, Lesson 26) → final norm → the lm_head projecting out logits. ModelRunner then calls sample() to hand logits to the Sampler (Lesson 28).

Look closer at why the dispatch is written this way. The first test, can_run_graph, is an and of three conditions: the mode must be graph-able (decode-like), the graph must actually have been recorded, and the current batch shape must fit one of the recorded buckets. Miss any one and replay is off — which is why graph replay sits up front and short-circuits: it is the "fast path", taken when possible, otherwise we fall back. After falling back, _prepare_eager_forward_batch does the eager path's normalization cleanup: padding for data-parallel (DP) across ranks, aligning tensor shapes so the following kernels run cleanly. Then it forks on is_extend — prefill honestly runs the whole batch, otherwise it lands in the eager decode fallback. Read this and you can pinpoint a "why didn't my decode use the CUDA graph" question to one of those three switches, instead of guessing in the dark.

Worth a closer look at what the attention layer does here: it takes the KV slots from ForwardBatch, writes the current token's K/V into the KV pool, then attends the current Q against the historical K/V (possibly a prefix reused via RadixAttention, Lesson 7). In other words, the caching and reuse from Lessons 4–7 actually happen at this very moment when ModelRunner runs the forward — every intuition built in those earlier lessons gets "powered on" here for the first time. And precisely because the forward constantly reads/writes the KV pool and calls hardware-specific attention kernels, ModelRunner must hold model, KV pool, and attention backend all at once — none optional.

Walk the forward one notch finer. After the embedding looks input_ids up into token vectors, the data enters a stack of identically structured decoder layers. In each layer comes a norm, then the attention sub-layer: Q/K/V are projected by linear layers, K/V are written into the KV pool at out_cache_loc, and the attention kernel lets the current Q look at itself plus all historical K/V. If that prefix was already computed by another request and hung on the radix tree by RadixAttention (Lesson 7), those historical K/V need no recompute — they are reused directly; the cache reuse of Lessons 4–7 only lands on a concrete kernel right here. The attention output is residual-added, then through the MLP sub-layer (another norm + two linears + activation), another residual. Stack N such layers, do a final norm, and lm_head projects the last hidden vector to the full vocab size to get logits. One commonly missed optimization: decode only needs the logits at each request's last position (the next word only looks at the last step), so the forward usually slices "take the last token" before projecting, saving a lot of useless lm_head compute. The moment logits exist, ModelRunner immediately calls sample(), where the next lesson's Sampler enters — the logits→token turning point of the whole pipeline happens right here.

1

Embed

input_ids → token vectors; positions ready for RoPE.

2

N decoder layers

each: attention (read/write KV pool) + MLP + norms (Lesson 26).

3

Final norm + lm_head

last hidden → vocab-sized logits.

4

Sample

sample() → Sampler emits the next token (Lesson 28).

EXTEND vs DECODE: one forward, two personalities

Remember the two phases from Lesson 4? They become two forwards here in ModelRunner:

EXTEND / prefill forward

Processes many tokens of the whole prompt at once (variable length), compute-bound, highly parallel; shapes vary, so it mostly runs eager or piecewise/chunked (Lesson 22). It fills this batch's K/V into the cache.

DECODE / decode forward

Each request computes just 1 new token — regular shapes, fixed batch — perfect for CUDA graph replay (Lesson 27), erasing the launch overhead of hundreds of tiny kernels. It appends one row to the cache.

Fig 24·2 · one batch holds both kinds — one EXTEND/prefill (200 tokens) and three DECODE/decode (1 token each) are packed into a single ForwardBatch; input_ids length = 200+1+1+1 = 203, consumed by one forward pass.

A concrete example: pack 3 decode requests (each computing just 1 new token) and 1 prefill of 200 tokens into one ForwardBatch, and input_ids has length 200 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 203. The prefill segment's forward_mode is EXTEND (200 tokens fed at once), while the three decodes are DECODE (one appended token each); they share a single forward pass and one set of attention metadata, computed by the GPU in one shot — exactly how continuous batching (Lesson 21) runs prefill and decode mixed together.

Why is it decode that suits CUDA graphs while prefill doesn't? It comes down to "is the shape stable". A CUDA graph records a fixed sequence of kernel calls at fixed memory addresses; once recorded, replay can only apply the same tensor shapes and same pointers. Decode produces exactly 1 new token per request per step, the batch size stays fixed for a while, and each sequence grows by just 1 — highly regular shapes — so varying real lengths can be padded into a few fixed buckets, each recorded once and replayed many times. And decode is precisely the phase where launch overhead dominates (little compute per step, lots of kernel dispatch), so erasing it pays the most. Prefill is the opposite: prompt lengths vary wildly and hundreds-to-thousands of tokens are computed at once, so shapes differ almost every batch; recording a graph per shape is neither worthwhile nor feasible, so prefill prefers the eager path or a chunked/piecewise compromise (Lessons 22, 27). In one line: decode is "small, regular, repeated millions of times" — right in the sweet spot of graph replay; prefill is "big, varied, each its own", better carried by the flexible eager path.

These two personalities really reflect two bottlenecks. Prefill processes hundreds-to-thousands of tokens at once with high arithmetic intensity, nearly saturating the GPU's compute units — classically compute-bound; here launch overhead is negligible next to the math, graph or not makes little difference, and the real optimization is making those matmuls big and full. Decode computes just 1 token per request per step, with thin, small matrices, so the GPU spends much of its time moving data (reading weights, reading KV) — classically memory-bound; now each kernel is tiny, and the fixed cost of launch/dispatch becomes the bulk, so flattening hundreds of kernel launches with a CUDA graph pays off immediately. Once you see "prefill lacks compute, decode lacks bandwidth", you understand why one forward splits into two paths, and why Lesson 27 mostly applies graph replay to decode. This "compute vs bandwidth" dividing line reappears again and again when we get to attention kernels, quantization, and speculative decoding — a universal yardstick running through the whole of inference optimization.

This also explains why ModelRunner also owns: the CUDA-graph runner (Lesson 27), the attention backend (Lesson 33), the KV pool (Lesson 30), and a draft model for speculative decoding (Lesson 43) — it is the assembly shop for these GPU-side parts. Next, Lesson 25 shows how its model gets loaded, and Lesson 26 shows how that model is written.

Think of ModelRunner as a GPU-side assembly machine: it bolts scattered parts into one running pipeline. The model gives it the "what to compute" structure, the KV pool gives it the "where history lives" memory, the attention backend gives it the "how to do attention on this hardware" craft, the CUDA-graph runner gives it the "how to run decode cheaper" overdrive, and the draft model (Lesson 43) gives it the "guess a few, verify in one shot" parallel gear for speculative decoding. Each part has its own lesson, but they all hang on ModelRunner and are strung together by the same forward. Because the parts converge here, debugging a "forward computed wrong / ran slow" issue almost always starts at ModelRunner: first check forward_mode went the right way, then check the attention metadata and KV slots, finally check whether a shape-mismatched CUDA graph was wrongly applied. Keep this assembly machine's internal wiring in your head, and the next four lessons are just taking apart its parts one by one — each one you remove makes your picture of this forward more three-dimensional, until you see the whole pipeline as one.

Place this lesson back in the whole map: Lessons 1–8 tell you "why it's designed this way", Lessons 13–17 cover how requests "get in", Lessons 18–23 cover how the scheduler "decides" — and this lesson is the instant where a decision lands as compute. From here, the rest of Part 6 unfolds along the few things in ModelRunner's hands: how model weights are loaded onto the card (Lesson 25), how a model file is written (Lesson 26, with Llama as the example), why decode can use a CUDA graph to flatten launch overhead (Lesson 27), and the Sampler turning logits into a token (Lesson 28). Carry the key — "ModelRunner = the decide→compute boundary, one per rank, holding model + KV pool + attention backend" — and the next four lessons read smoothly.

📌 Key points
  • ModelRunner: one per TP rank, the decide→compute boundary; holds model + KV pool, forward→logits, sample→token.
  • ForwardBatch: the GPU view of a ScheduleBatch (L19) — ids/positions/forward_mode/attn metadata/KV pointers.
  • One forward: embed → N decoder layers → norm + lm_head → logits → Sampler.
  • Two forwards: EXTEND (prefill, compute-bound, mostly eager) vs DECODE (regular shapes, CUDA-graph replay, L27).
  • Assembly shop: ModelRunner also owns the CUDA graph, attention backend, KV pool, draft model — the rest of Part 6 unpacks them.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. In SGLang, what is the responsibility boundary between ModelRunner and the scheduler (Lesson 18)?
  1. ModelRunner receives HTTP requests and tokenizes; the scheduler does the forward
  2. The scheduler only decides, never computes (receive, batch, set policy) and calls run_batch each step to dispatch work; ModelRunner is what actually turns a batch of tokens into logits on the GPU — it is the decide→compute boundary, one per TP rank, owned by the TpWorker
  3. The scheduler both decides and runs the forward itself; ModelRunner only samples the token
  4. The whole process shares one ModelRunner that all TP ranks call
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. Part 5 is all about how the scheduler decides; it never touches the GPU. What turns tokens into logits is ModelRunner. The chain is run_batch → TpWorker → the one ModelRunner it owns, one per TP rank, each computing a slice and stitching via collectives. So it is the decide→compute boundary, not a shared singleton, and it doesn't ingest requests/tokenize.
2. What is the relationship between ForwardBatch and ScheduleBatch (Lesson 19)?
  1. ForwardBatch is the sampling result; ScheduleBatch is the input prompt
  2. ForwardBatch is the GPU view of a ScheduleBatch — the same batch re-expressed as GPU tensors/metadata: input_ids, positions (for RoPE), forward_mode (picks the path), attention metadata + out_cache_loc (KV read/write slots); ScheduleBatch records 'who needs computing', ForwardBatch records 'how the GPU computes this step'
  3. They are identical, just renamed
  4. ScheduleBatch lives on GPU and ForwardBatch on CPU, mirroring each other
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. ModelRunner first translates the scheduler's ScheduleBatch (a set of Reqs) into a ForwardBatch — exactly what the GPU forward needs: which tokens, each token's position, prefill vs decode, attention backend and KV slots. In short ScheduleBatch='who needs computing', ForwardBatch='how the GPU computes this step'. It is not a sampling result, a mere rename, or a CPU mirror.
3. Why does the DECODE forward suit CUDA-graph replay (Lesson 27) while EXTEND/prefill mostly runs eager?
  1. Because decode doesn't read the KV cache, it can be graphed
  2. Because prefill must run on CPU and can't use a GPU graph
  3. Because decode is more compute-heavy than prefill and more worth optimizing
  4. Decode emits just 1 new token per request per step with highly regular shapes (paddable to a few fixed buckets), and with little compute per step the launch overhead dominates, so replay flattens that overhead; prefill has wildly varying lengths, different shapes every batch, and is already compute-bound, so recording graphs is neither worthwhile nor feasible
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. A CUDA graph records a fixed-shape, fixed-address kernel sequence; replay only fits the same shapes. Decode is 'small, regular, repeated millions of times', memory-bound with launch overhead dominating, so replay pays most; prefill is 'big, varied, each its own', compute-bound with shapes differing nearly every batch, better on the eager path. Decode does read KV, prefill doesn't run on CPU, and decode does less compute per step.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • Using the 'machine operator on the floor' analogy, fully explain how one forward runs through ModelRunner. Cover each: (1) the call chain — scheduler run_batch (Lesson 18) → TpWorker → the one-per-rank ModelRunner, and why 8 ranks must run the same batch in lockstep (collectives deadlock otherwise); (2) ModelRunner first translates ScheduleBatch into ForwardBatch, and what positions/forward_mode/attention metadata/out_cache_loc each drive; (3) the forward internals embed → N decoder layers (attention reads/writes the KV pool, reusing a RadixAttention prefix on a hit, Lesson 7) → final norm + lm_head → logits, and where the logits→token turning point (sample() handing off to the Lesson 28 Sampler) happens.
  • Centered on EXTEND vs DECODE, fully explain the 'two personalities of one forward' and how it shapes the rest of Part 6. Explain: (1) their differences in token count, shape regularity, and bottleneck (prefill compute-bound vs decode memory-bound); (2) why decode suits CUDA-graph replay (Lesson 27) while prefill mostly runs eager/chunked (Lesson 22); (3) why ModelRunner must hold the model (Lessons 25/26), KV pool (Lesson 30), attention backend (Lesson 33), and even a draft model (Lesson 43) all at once, and from that, which parts the rest of Part 6 takes apart one by one.

Model loading & weights

Last lesson's ModelRunner casually asked for "the loaded model" — but how does that model actually move from disk onto the GPU? A 70B's weights are hundreds of GB, scattered across dozens of files, still named the HuggingFace way, not matching SGLang's internal modules. This lesson is about the backstage role that turns "a pile of disk files" into "tensors in their right place on each card": DefaultModelLoader.

🔌 Analogy
Think of loading a model as furnishing a new home from numbered flat-pack boxes: first read the parts list (the HuggingFace weight names, e.g. model.layers.0.self_attn.q_proj.weight), match each part to the right room (the corresponding SGLang module param), cut each shelf to fit when needed (slice it into this rank's share under tensor parallelism), and assemble in the right finish (cast to bf16/fp16, or load a quantized format). The key: don't dump all the boxes into the living room at once — carry them in one box at a time, streamed, or host RAM blows up on the spot. That "claim-by-list, cut-to-fit, stream-assemble" is exactly the Loader's job.
🌍 The big picture
DefaultModelLoader.load_model is the common entry for almost every model: it first builds an empty shell from model_config (structure present, weights still placeholders), then opens the safetensors shards on disk and streams the tensors in one by one. The feeding is handled by each model's own load_weights(weights): it knows which internal param an HF name maps to, which weights to fuse, and which slice this rank takes. Because loading is funneled through this one point, SGLang can support hundreds of models + many quant formats under one framework.

load_model: build the shell first, then stream the weights in

The whole load path is just two steps, but each hides nuance. Step one: within the target device (GPU) and target dtype context, _initialize_model instantiates the structure — all the linear layers, attention, and embeddings are built, but the params are still uninitialized placeholder tensors (already allocated at full size on the GPU, just not yet filled with real values). Step two: call load_weights_and_postprocess, which gets a weight iterator from _get_all_weights and hands it to model.load_weights(weights) to pour the real values in, tensor by tensor. Note the subtlety: weights are not "read fully into memory then distributed" — they are a (name, tensor) generator: read one, load one, drop one, so host memory stays pinned to a small upper bound even for a hundreds-of-GB model.

Why insist on streaming? Picture an 8-GPU box loading a 70B: if you read all weights into host RAM before slicing and dispatching, that temporary copy alone could approach or exceed physical memory, and safetensors' zero-copy advantage is wasted. Streaming makes "read—map—slice—move-to-card" a read-and-drop pipeline: at any instant only the current tensor (and the target slice being processed) lives in memory, so the bound is decoupled from the model's total size. That's why the same machine can load a few-GB small model and a hundreds-of-GB large one — memory pressure is nearly constant. Once you see this, you understand why SGLang defaults to safetensors, and why "slow loading" is usually disk/network bandwidth, not a memory bottleneck.

1

Locate the checkpoint

Find the local or downloaded dir from model_path (HF / ModelScope).

2

safetensors shards

Open multiple *.safetensors by index; zero-copy, readable tensor-by-tensor.

3

Name mapping

HF weight name →(the model's own load_weights)→ SGLang internal param, often fusing q/k/v, gate/up.

4

Slice for TP

Each weight split column/row-wise; this rank takes only its slice (Lessons 24/46).

5

dtype / quant

Cast to bf16/fp16, or load FP8/INT4/AWQ/GPTQ with scales (Lesson 35).

6

Land on GPU

Write into the shell model's param slots; once loaded, model.eval().

Fig 1 · Load flow — safetensors shards on disk are streamed out by the Loader; each tensor is cast to dtype or arranged by the quant layout with scales, then only each rank's slice is scattered into its own GPU VRAM.

Name mapping & fusion: matching HF names to SGLang params
